The first layer is **SubBytes**, the byte substitution: each of the 16 bytes is replaced via an **S-box**. Here the first major difference from DES appears — AES has only *one* single S-box (8 bits in, 8 bits out), and it's not a random-looking table but a clean mathematical operation over the finite field GF(2⁸), consisting of an inversion and an affine mapping. SubBytes is the **only nonlinear** element of AES and thus provides all of the **confusion**.

![AES layer SubBytes: each byte of the State is replaced via the S-box](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a4/AES-SubBytes.svg "SubBytes: each of the 16 State bytes passes through the same AES S-box. This is the only nonlinear element and provides the confusion.")

The second layer is **ShiftRows**: the four rows of the State matrix are cyclically shifted — the first stays put, the second by one, the third by two, the fourth by three positions. This mixes bytes across the column boundaries and provides **diffusion**.

![AES layer ShiftRows: the rows of the State are cyclically shifted](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/66/AES-ShiftRows.svg "ShiftRows: each row of the State matrix is cyclically shifted by a growing number of positions — a diffusion layer.")

The third layer is **MixColumns**: each column of the State is multiplied by a fixed 4×4 matrix (in the Galois field), which thoroughly mixes the four bytes of a column. ShiftRows and MixColumns together produce the diffusion, and they do it so effectively that after just **two rounds** every single State byte depends on *all 16* plaintext bytes.

![AES layer MixColumns: each column is multiplied by a fixed matrix](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/76/AES-MixColumns.svg "MixColumns: each column of the State is multiplied by a fixed matrix and thereby mixed — the second diffusion layer.")

The fourth layer is **AddRoundKey**: the State is XORed byte by byte with the current round key — only here does the key secret enter. Two subtleties you should remember, because they're often asked: AES uses **key whitening**, meaning there's an additional AddRoundKey already *before* the first round and one at the end, so that the attacker never sees an unencrypted intermediate state. And the **very last round leaves out MixColumns** — this makes decryption more symmetric without changing security. The 16 round keys are generated by a word-based **key schedule** (one word is 32 bits): a nonlinear g-function (rotation, S-box substitution, addition of a round constant) feeds recursively through the words. AES is thereby very fast in both software and hardware — Intel processors even have their own instruction set **AES-NI** for it — and the NSA approves AES-128 for SECRET and AES-192/256 for TOP SECRET. [Spanning Tree animates the whole round very clearly](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C4ATDMIz5wc).

> **Mnemonic (AES round):** **S – S – M – A** = **S**ubBytes → **S**hiftRows → **M**ixColumns → **A**ddRoundKey. Only the *first* S (SubBytes) is nonlinear and provides confusion; ShiftRows and MixColumns are diffusion. And: the **last round without MixColumns**.

## Modes of operation: how to encrypt more than one block

AES encrypts exactly 16 bytes at a time, DES exactly 8. But real messages are longer. A **mode of operation** determines how you apply the block cipher repeatedly to encrypt a message of arbitrary length — and the choice of mode decides between security and disaster.

The naive mode is called **ECB (Electronic Codebook)**: you split the message into blocks, encrypt each one individually, and concatenate the ciphertexts; if the last block doesn't fit exactly, you fill it with padding. This sounds harmless but has a fatal flaw: ECB is **deterministic**. The same plaintext block always yields the same ciphertext under the same key. If you encrypt an image with it, all areas of the same color remain visible as the same patterns in the ciphertext too — the most famous example is the Linux penguin Tux, whose outlines are still clearly recognizable after ECB encryption:

![The original image of the penguin Tux before encryption](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/56/Tux.jpg "Tux in the original — in a moment we'll see what ECB mode makes of it.")

![Tux encrypted in ECB mode — the outlines remain clearly visible](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f0/Tux_ecb.jpg "Tux after ECB encryption: because identical plaintext blocks are always encrypted identically, the pattern is preserved. That is exactly why ECB is insecure.")

Worse still: because each block is encrypted on its own, an attacker can swap or reorder entire ciphertext blocks without knowing the key — for example, copy his own encrypted account number into the block of someone else's transfer. The lesson: **never use ECB for real data.**

The rescue is **probabilistic** modes, which use a random **initialization vector (IV)** to ensure that identical plaintexts yield different ciphertexts. The most important is **CBC (Cipher Block Chaining)**: before encryption, each plaintext block is chained via XOR with the *previous ciphertext block*, i.e. yᵢ = eₖ(xᵢ ⊕ yᵢ₋₁); the very first block is XORed with the IV: y₁ = eₖ(x₁ ⊕ IV). This way each block feeds into the next, and identical plaintext blocks are encrypted differently depending on their history. The third mode, **OFB (Output Feedback)**, turns the block cipher into a stream cipher: from the IV you generate a keystream (s₁ = eₖ(IV), sᵢ = eₖ(sᵢ₋₁)) and apply it to the plaintext via XOR (yᵢ = sᵢ ⊕ xᵢ).

![Cipher Block Chaining (CBC): each block is chained with the previous ciphertext before encryption](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/80/CBC_encryption.svg "CBC: each plaintext block is XORed with the previous ciphertext, the first with the initialization vector — this makes encryption probabilistic.")

### Step by step: ECB, CBC, and OFB by hand

This is exactly what the exercise sheet computes, so let's do it once in full. We take the tiny block cipher with 5-bit blocks that merely reorders the bits: **e(b₁b₂b₃b₄b₅) = (b₂b₅b₄b₁b₃)**. The plaintext is x = **01101 11011 11010 00110**, and for CBC and OFB the IV = **11001**.

**ECB** encrypts each block on its own. For the first block 01101, b₁=0, b₂=1, b₃=1, b₄=0, b₅=1, so e(01101) = (b₂,b₅,b₄,b₁,b₃) = 11001. The same for the other three blocks: 11011 → 11110, 11010 → 10110, 00110 → 00101. The ciphertext is **y = 11001 11110 10110 00101**.

**CBC** chains. First x₁ ⊕ IV = 01101 ⊕ 11001 = 10100, then e(10100) = 00011, so y₁ = **00011**. The second block: x₂ ⊕ y₁ = 11011 ⊕ 00011 = 11000, then e(11000) = 10010, so y₂ = **10010**. Already here you see the effect — even though the first and no other block ever look the same, each ciphertext depends on everything before it.

**OFB** first generates a keystream from the IV. s₁ = e(IV) = e(11001) = 11010, and y₁ = s₁ ⊕ x₁ = 11010 ⊕ 01101 = **10111**. Then s₂ = e(s₁) = e(11010) = 10110, and y₂ = s₂ ⊕ x₂ = 10110 ⊕ 11011 = **01101**. Here the block cipher is only used to generate random bits — encryption happens as in a stream cipher, via XOR.

> **Mnemonic (ECB penguin):** if you can still recognize the penguin in the ciphertext, it was ECB. **Same block → same ciphertext** = deterministic = insecure. CBC and OFB scramble that using the IV.

## The wall of symmetric cryptography

As strong as AES is — it shares three built-in weaknesses with every symmetric cipher, and those are exactly what motivate the next great leap. The first and most serious is the **key exchange problem**: Alice and Bob need the same secret key, but the only channel between them is the internet — and that's exactly where Oskar is eavesdropping. If Alice sends the key over this channel, Oskar has it immediately. The second weakness is the **number of keys**: every pair of participants needs its own secret key, and that grows quadratically — with 120 employees that's already 120·119/2 = 7140 keys that all have to be generated, distributed, and kept secret. The third weakness is the lack of **non-repudiation**: because both sides possess the same key, in a dispute you cannot prove *which* of the two generated a message — both could have done it.

## Asymmetric cryptography: the public mailbox

The solution is so elegant that on its publication in 1976 (by Whitfield Diffie, Martin Hellman, and Ralph Merkle; the RSA method followed in 1977, and Britain's intelligence service already knew the principle in 1972) it seemed like a magic trick. Instead of a single secret key, each person gets a **key pair** consisting of a public key k_pub and a private key k_pr. And now the astonishing part: **the key for encrypting is not secret.** The best image for this is a public mailbox. Anyone may drop in a letter — that corresponds to encrypting with the public key, which the whole world is allowed to know. But emptying it, i.e. decrypting, can only be done by whoever possesses the private key. So if Bob wants to send Alice something secret, he encrypts it with *Alice's* public key; only Alice can read it again with her private key — and the key exchange problem vanishes into thin air, because the public key may travel over the wiretapped channel without worry.

![Asymmetric encryption: encrypt with the public key, decrypt with the private one](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f0/Orange_blue_public_key_cryptography_en.svg "Asymmetric: encryption is done with the recipient's public key, decryption only with their private key. The public mailbox in one image.")

For this to work at all, every asymmetric method needs a **one-way function** — a function whose forward direction y = f(x) is easy to compute, but whose inverse x = f⁻¹(y) remains practically impossible. Two such functions carry the entire public-key world: the **factorization of large numbers** (you multiply two huge primes effortlessly, but decomposing the product back into its factors is hopeless) — RSA is built on this — and the **discrete logarithm**, on which Diffie-Hellman and elliptic curves are based. There are essentially no more than these three big families (RSA, Diffie-Hellman, elliptic curves).

> **Mnemonic (public vs. private):** **encrypt with public, decrypt with private** — like a mailbox: anyone may drop in, only the owner may empty it.

### Step by step: generating and using an RSA key pair

RSA implements the factorization one-way function in five steps, and the exercise sheet has you compute exactly that with small numbers. The five steps of key generation are: **(1)** choose two large primes p and q; **(2)** form their product N = p·q; **(3)** compute Euler's totient T = (p−1)·(q−1); **(4)** choose two numbers e and d such that (e·d) mod T = 1 (where e must be coprime to T and less than T); **(5)** set the public key as the pair (N, e) and the private one as (N, d). Encryption is then done with y = xᵉ mod N and decryption with x = yᵈ mod N (for any plaintext value x < N).

Let's compute this with the tiny exercise values p = 2, q = 7. Then N = 2·7 = 14 and T = (2−1)·(7−1) = 6. We choose e = 5 (coprime to 6) and look for d with (5·d) mod 6 = 1 — that is d = 11, because 5·11 = 55 and 55 mod 6 = 1. So the **public key is (14, 5)** and the **private key is (14, 11)**. Let's encrypt the message **BCD**, i.e. using the letter table the numbers B = 1, C = 2, D = 3, each with y = x⁵ mod 14:

- B = 1: 1⁵ = 1, and 1 mod 14 = **1**.
- C = 2: 2⁵ = 32, and 32 mod 14 = **4**.
- D = 3: 3⁵ = 243, and 243 mod 14 = **5** (because 17·14 = 238).

The ciphertext is therefore the number sequence **1, 4, 5**. As a check, let's decrypt with x = y¹¹ mod 14: 1¹¹ mod 14 = 1 (B), 4¹¹ mod 14 = 2 (C), and 5¹¹ mod 14 = 3 (D) — we end up back at BCD. (When computing the powers, you of course reduce modulo 14 after each step, exactly the clever technique from the second chapter.) *Why* RSA works — why encrypting with e and decrypting with d exactly cancel out — that is the mathematics of the next chapter.

## Why in the end you need both: hybrid protocols

There's a catch that explains why asymmetric cryptography has *not* replaced symmetric: it is **dramatically slower**. Work it out using the example of a 1 GB video. At a typical RSA speed of 100 kbit/s, you'd need around 80,000 seconds, i.e. over 22 hours. The same video with AES at 17 Mbit/s is done in about 8 minutes. So asymmetric methods are orders of magnitude more expensive, and for the same security they also need much longer keys — 128 bits symmetric correspond roughly to 3072 bits in RSA. That's why you never compare key lengths between the two worlds.

| symmetric | elliptic curves | RSA / Diffie-Hellman |
|---|---|---|
| 64 bits | 128 bits | 700 bits |
| 128 bits | 256 bits | 3072 bits |

Practice therefore takes the best of both worlds and combines them in a **hybrid protocol**, as SSL/TLS does behind every "https": you use the slow asymmetric cryptography only once at the beginning, to securely exchange a fresh symmetric session key — and afterwards encrypt the actual, large amounts of data quickly and symmetrically with AES. This way the asymmetric part solves the key exchange problem, and the symmetric part provides the speed.

## In a nutshell

The short version of the three acts: **AES** replaced DES and is built differently — 128-bit blocks, keys of 128/192/256 bits, 10/12/14 rounds, and instead of Feistel it works with four layers that process the whole block each round: **SubBytes** (the only nonlinear layer, confusion), **ShiftRows** and **MixColumns** (diffusion), and **AddRoundKey** (introducing the key via XOR), with key whitening and without MixColumns in the last round. For messages longer than one block, you need a **mode of operation**: **ECB** (deterministic, therefore insecure — the penguin stays visible), **CBC** (chains each block via XOR with the previous ciphertext plus IV), or **OFB** (turns the block cipher into a stream cipher). Symmetric hits three limits — key exchange, quadratically many keys, no non-repudiation — which **asymmetric cryptography** solves with a public/private key pair and a **one-way function** (factorization in RSA, discrete logarithm in Diffie-Hellman/ECC). Because asymmetric is much slower, practice combines both **hybrid** (TLS).

## Terms & Notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **AES / Rijndael** | layered block cipher, 128-bit block, 10/12/14 rounds |
| **State** | 4×4 byte matrix on which AES operates |
| **SubBytes** | S-box substitution; only nonlinear element (confusion) |
| **ShiftRows / MixColumns** | shift rows / mix columns (diffusion) |
| **AddRoundKey** | XOR of the State with the round key |
| **Key whitening** | additional AddRoundKey at the beginning and end |
| **ECB / CBC / OFB** | modes of operation: deterministic / chained / stream-like |
| **IV** | initialization vector — makes a mode probabilistic |
| **One-way function** | easy forward, practically impossible backward |
| **RSA key pair** | public (N, e), private (N, d); y = xᵉ mod N |
| **Hybrid protocol** | asymmetric key exchange + symmetric data (TLS) |
| **Non-repudiation** | only possible asymmetrically |

## Common pitfalls

- **AES is a Feistel cipher? No** — AES uses layers and encrypts the whole block each round; only DES is Feistel.
- **AES has eight S-boxes like DES? No** — AES has exactly **one** S-box, mathematically defined over GF(2⁸).
- **ECB is okay for short or "unimportant" data? No** — the determinism is always a problem; never use ECB for real data.
- **The last AES round is like all the others? No** — it lacks MixColumns.
- **Asymmetric replaces symmetric? No** — it's much slower; practice uses both hybrid.
- **Same bit length = same security? No** — 128 bits symmetric correspond roughly to 3072 bits RSA. Don't compare key lengths between the worlds.

## Exam focus

The exercise sheet clearly shows where computation happens. The first big block is the **modes of operation**: you get a small block cipher (say a 5-bit permutation) and are asked to encrypt a multi-part message in **ECB, CBC, and OFB** — you must apply the formulas confidently (ECB block by block, CBC with yᵢ = eₖ(xᵢ ⊕ yᵢ₋₁) and the IV for the first block, OFB with the keystream generated from the IV) and be able to explain why ECB is insecure and what the IV is for. The second block is **RSA by hand**: generate a key pair following the five steps (N = p·q, T = (p−1)(q−1), e·d ≡ 1 mod T), then encrypt with y = xᵉ mod N and decrypt again with x = yᵈ mod N — say the message BCD with (N=14, e=5, d=11). Add to that two comparison computations: the **number of keys** in a company (purely symmetric n·(n−1)/2, asymmetric only n pairs — so with 120 employees, 7140 versus 120) and a **performance estimate** (1 GB file at given AES and RSA speeds, from which it follows why you work hybrid). On the knowledge side: the **four AES layers** in order (only SubBytes nonlinear, last round without MixColumns), distinguish **DES from AES**, and explain the **one-way function** with its two practical examples (factorization, discrete logarithm) as well as the three families RSA/DH/ECC.

## More on this

- **Computerphile — AES Explained** (EN, Mike Pound): the AES idea and its layers illustrated vividly. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O4xNJsjtN6E
- **Spanning Tree — AES: How to Design Secure Encryption** (~13 min, EN): from confusion/diffusion to the complete round, very clearly animated. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C4ATDMIz5wc
- **Computerphile — Public Key Cryptography** (~6 min, EN): why a "public" key can work — the intuition behind one-way functions. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GSIDS_lvRv4
expl:cs-2025-l05:content:
RSA is the first concrete asymmetric scheme — and by far the most exam-relevant computational topic in the entire course. The common thread is surprisingly simple: from two prime numbers you build a key pair, you encrypt a message by raising it to an exponent and reducing it modulo n, and you decrypt with the matching „counter-power". The whole security rests on a single fact: you can multiply two large primes effortlessly, but you practically cannot decompose the product back into its factors. To make this even computable in practice, you need two tools that we also work through by hand here — the extended Euclidean algorithm (it delivers the private key) and square-and-multiply (it makes the enormous powers computable). And right at the end we turn RSA around and get the **digital signature** for free — the mathematical signature that proves who wrote a message.

## The basic idea: encrypt publicly, decrypt privately

RSA inverts the symmetric logic. Everyone knows your public key, a pair of numbers (n, e), and can use it to send you encrypted messages: the encryption of a plaintext value x is **y = xᵉ mod n**, the decryption of the ciphertext y is **x = yᵈ mod n** with your secret private key d. Both operations are at their core the same — „a number raised to an exponent, modulo n". The whole magic lies in the fact that e and d are chosen together so that raising to the d *exactly* undoes raising to the e. Whoever knows d decrypts effortlessly; whoever only has the public (n, e) would have to factorize the number n to compute d — and that is precisely what is impossible for large n.

![Asymmetric encryption: encrypt with the public key, decrypt with the private one](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f9/Public_key_encryption.svg "RSA encryption: Bob encrypts with Alice's public key (n, e), only Alice decrypts with her private d.")

## RSA key generation in five steps

Key generation is a fixed sequence that you must know in your sleep. **First**, you choose two large primes p and q — by far the most expensive step, because for it you draw candidates with a random generator and check them with a primality test (such as the Fermat test) until two primes are found. **Second**, you compute their product n = p·q; this n is the public modulus, and its bit length is roughly the sum of the bit lengths of p and q (two 1024-bit primes yield a ~2048-bit n). **Third**, you compute Euler's phi function φ(n) = (p−1)·(q−1). **Fourth**, you choose the public exponent e from the range 1 to φ(n)−1 such that e is coprime to φ(n), that is gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1. **Fifth**, you compute the private key d such that d·e ≡ 1 mod φ(n) — in other words, d is the **modular inverse** of e modulo φ(n). What is published in the end is the pair (n, e); d as well as p, q and φ(n) remain secret.

> **Mnemonic (the 5 steps):** **p, q → n → φ → e → d.** Two primes, their product n, from it φ = (p−1)(q−1), then an e with gcd(e, φ) = 1, and finally d as the inverse of e modulo φ.

### Step by step: a complete RSA example

Let's work everything through with small numbers, exactly as in the exam. We take **p = 3** and **q = 11**. Then **n = 3·11 = 33** and **φ(n) = (3−1)·(11−1) = 2·10 = 20**. As the public exponent we choose **e = 3**, since gcd(3, 20) = 1. Now we look for d with 3·d ≡ 1 mod 20 — by trial or with the algorithm that follows shortly, one finds **d = 7**, since 3·7 = 21 = 20 + 1 ≡ 1 mod 20. So the public key is **(33, 3)**, the private one **(33, 7)**.

Let's encrypt the plaintext value **x = 4**: y = 4³ mod 33 = 64 mod 33 = **31**. And let's decrypt as a check: x = 31⁷ mod 33. A neat computational trick saves work here — 31 ≡ −2 mod 33, so 31⁷ ≡ (−2)⁷ = −128, and −128 mod 33 = −128 + 4·33 = **4**. We land back at the plaintext 4. Note the condition x < n: every plaintext value must be smaller than the modulus, otherwise the unique inversion is lost.

## Why it works: Euler's phi function

The key to *understanding* (not just to computing) is **Euler's phi function φ(n)**: it counts how many numbers from 1 to n are coprime to n. Two rules carry the whole of RSA. First: if n is itself a prime, then *all* n−1 smaller numbers are coprime to n, so φ(n) = n−1 (for example φ(7) = 6). Second: if p and q are both prime, then φ(p·q) = φ(p)·φ(q) = (p−1)·(q−1). And here is the decisive point that makes RSA secure: φ(n) can be computed easily *only* if you know p and q. An attacker sees only n; to obtain φ(n) from it, he would have to decompose n into p and q — and without φ(n) there is no d. The security of RSA is therefore exactly the difficulty of **factorization**.

### Step by step: finding d with the extended Euclidean algorithm

The fifth step — determining d as the inverse of e — is done systematically with the **extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA)**. For two numbers it computes not only their greatest common divisor but also two coefficients s and t such that gcd(φ(n), e) = s·φ(n) + t·e. If this gcd equals 1 (which is the case for a valid e), then **t is exactly the sought d** — the inverse of e modulo φ(n). The algorithm runs iteratively: at each step you form the remainder rᵢ = rᵢ₋₂ mod rᵢ₋₁, the quotient qᵢ₋₁ = (rᵢ₋₂ − rᵢ)/rᵢ₋₁ and carry s and t along via the rules sᵢ = sᵢ₋₂ − qᵢ₋₁·sᵢ₋₁ and tᵢ = tᵢ₋₂ − qᵢ₋₁·tᵢ₋₁, until the remainder becomes 0. Let's do this for our example with φ(n) = 20 and e = 3:

| i | rᵢ | qᵢ₋₁ | sᵢ | tᵢ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 20 | — | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 3 | — | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 20 mod 3 = 2 | 6 | 1 − 6·0 = 1 | 0 − 6·1 = −6 |
| 3 | 3 mod 2 = 1 | 1 | 0 − 1·1 = −1 | 1 − 1·(−6) = 7 |
| 4 | 2 mod 1 = 0 | — | — | — |

The last nonzero remainder is r₃ = 1 = gcd(20, 3), so e is valid, and t₃ = **7** is our d. As a check: s·20 + t·3 = (−1)·20 + 7·3 = −20 + 21 = 1. Correct — and 7 is indeed the inverse of 3 modulo 20.

## Square-and-multiply: computing enormous powers quickly

With real keys, the exponent is a 2048-bit number. Computing the power xᵉ naively by repeated multiplication would require an astronomical number of steps — for a 1024-bit exponent on the order of 10³⁰⁰ multiplications, more than there are atoms in the observable universe. **Square-and-multiply** achieves the same with only about one and a half operations *per bit* of the exponent, by working through the **binary representation of the exponent**, from the most significant bit (MSB) to the least significant (LSB). The rule is as simple as can be: at the first bit you simply take the base. At every further bit you square the previous intermediate result — and *additionally*, if the bit is a 1, you multiply once more by the base. After each step you reduce modulo n, so that the numbers stay small (exactly the reduction technique from the second chapter).

### Step by step: square-and-multiply by hand

Let's compute the decryption 31⁷ mod 33 with square-and-multiply. The exponent 7 in binary is **111**, so three ones. We work through them from the left:

| Bit (from the left) | Operation | Computation mod 33 | Intermediate result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (MSB) | take the base | — | 31 |
| 1 | square, then × base | 31² = 961 ≡ 4; 4·31 = 124 ≡ 25 | 25 |
| 1 (LSB) | square, then × base | 25² = 625 ≡ 31; 31·31 = 961 ≡ 4 | 4 |

Out comes **4** — exactly the plaintext we had already found above with the (−2) trick. For a bit 0, in the corresponding row you would *only* square and omit the multiplication.

> **Mnemonic (square-and-multiply):** Read the exponent **in binary from the left**. **Bit 1 → square *and* multiply (SM)**, **bit 0 → square only (S)**, the MSB is always just „write down the base". Write S or SM under each other for each bit — that is the computation path.

## RSA reversed: the digital signature

Now the elegant bonus that solves an entirely new protection goal. So far RSA has secured *confidentiality*. But if you reverse the order of the two keys, you get **non-repudiation** — the proof of *who* created a message. The procedure is called a digital signature. Alice **signs** a message m by „encrypting" it with her *private* key d: s = mᵈ mod n. This number s is the signature, and she sends off the pair (m, s). Anyone who knows Alice's *public* key e can **verify**: he computes sᵉ mod n and checks whether that again yields the message m. If it does, the signature can only come from Alice — for only she possesses d. Note the reversal of roles: when encrypting you use the *public* key of the recipient, when signing your *own private* one.

![Digital signature: sign with the private key, verify with the public one](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/99/Digital_Signature_diagram_de.svg "Digital signature: Alice signs with her private key, anyone verifies with her public one. If the check matches, the message is guaranteed to come from Alice.")

### Step by step: signing and verifying

Let's stay with our key pair (n = 33, e = 3, d = 7) and sign the message m = 4. Alice computes the signature s = mᵈ mod n = 4⁷ mod 33. With square-and-multiply or directly: 4⁷ = 16384, and 16384 mod 33 = 16 (since 496·33 = 16368). So s = **16**. Bob verifies with Alice's public e: sᵉ mod n = 16³ mod 33. Step by step: 16² = 256 ≡ 25 mod 33, then 25·16 = 400 ≡ 4 mod 33. Out comes **4** = m — the signature is valid, the message comes from Alice.

In practice, however, one does not sign whole documents directly, since they would be much larger than n. Instead one uses **hash signatures**: you first run the document through a cryptographic hash function H, which produces a short fingerprint x = H(m), and sign *only this fingerprint*: s = H(m)ᵈ mod n. The recipient recomputes the hash of the received message himself, decrypts the signature with the public key and compares the two values — if they agree, the document is unaltered *and* from Alice. (In the exercise sheet, the toy hash is the digit sum of the letter values: the hash of „ABC" would be 65+66+67 = 198; you then sign this 198 instead of the whole message.) What real hash functions look like and which properties they must have is the topic of the next chapter.

> **Mnemonic (encrypting vs. signing):** **Encrypting** = with the recipient's **public** key (only he reads). **Signing** = with your **own private** key (everyone checks, only you could do it). The two keys simply swap roles.

## Textbook RSA is dangerous: determinism and the ×2 attack

The pure formula we have used so far is called „textbook RSA", and as it stands it is insecure. First, it is **deterministic**: the same plaintext always yields the same ciphertext with the same key, so an attacker recognizes repetitions. Second, it is **malleable**, and in a particularly pretty way: Oskar sees the ciphertext y, computes 2ᵉ mod n himself and multiplies the two, thus forwarding y·2ᵉ mod n. On decryption the result is then not x but **2·x** — Oskar has deliberately doubled the plaintext without ever knowing it. The solution to both problems is **padding**: before encrypting, one builds random data into the plaintext, which makes RSA probabilistic and destroys malleability. Further attack surfaces you should know are faster factorization algorithms (there is progress, but for large n it remains hopeless — historically famous is the RSA-129 challenge) and **side-channel attacks**, in which, for instance, the power consumption during square-and-multiply reveals the private key. And not to forget: RSA is 100 to 1000 times slower than AES — another reason to use it only for key exchange and for signatures, never for bulk data.

## In a nutshell

The short version: RSA is the first concrete asymmetric scheme. The public key is **(n, e)**, the private one **d**; encryption is **y = xᵉ mod n**, decryption **x = yᵈ mod n**. You generate the key in five steps: two primes p, q → n = p·q → φ(n) = (p−1)(q−1) → an e with gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1 → d as the inverse of e modulo φ(n), determined with the **extended Euclidean algorithm**. The security rests solely on the difficulty of **factorizing** n — without p and q no φ(n), without φ(n) no d. To make the enormous powers computable, one uses **square-and-multiply** (binary exponent from the left, bit 1 = square and multiply, bit 0 = square only). If you reverse the keys, you sign: **s = mᵈ mod n**, verified with **sᵉ mod n** — in practice over the **hash** of the document. And „textbook RSA" without **padding** is deterministic and malleable through the ×2 trick.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **(n, e)** | public key: modulus n and exponent e |
| **d** | private key; d ≡ e⁻¹ mod φ(n) |
| **n = p·q** | product of two large primes |
| **φ(n)** | Euler's phi; for n = p·q it is φ(n) = (p−1)(q−1) |
| **gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1** | e coprime to φ(n) — condition for a valid e |
| **EEA** | extended Euclid: delivers the gcd *and* the inverse d |
| **square-and-multiply** | fast modular exponentiation over the bits of e |
| **signature** | s = mᵈ mod n (with private d), check sᵉ mod n |
| **hash signature** | not m but H(m) is signed |
| **textbook RSA** | RSA without padding — deterministic and malleable |

## Common pitfalls

- **φ(n) = n − 1? Only if n is prime.** In RSA n = p·q, so φ(n) = (p−1)(q−1).
- **e freely choosable? Almost** — but gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1 must hold, otherwise no matching d exists.
- **d easily computable from n and e? Only with φ(n)** — and that presupposes the factorization of n, which is precisely the hard problem.
- **In square-and-multiply, compute modulo only at the end? No** — reduce after *every* step, otherwise the intermediate results explode.
- **Signing = encrypting with the public key? No, the other way around** — signing is done with the *private* key, verifying with the public one.
- **Textbook RSA is secure enough? No** — without padding it is deterministic and malleable through the ×2 trick.

## Exam focus

RSA is *the* computational topic, and the exercise sheet demonstrates it. First a **complete RSA example with small numbers**: the five steps of key generation (p, q → n → φ(n) → e with gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1 → d with e·d ≡ 1 mod φ(n)), then **encrypt a message** (often letters via the A=0…Z=25 table) with y = xᵉ mod n **and decrypt it again with x = yᵈ mod n**. You must be able to **complete a given key pair or recognize it as invalid** (e.g. check whether e is coprime to φ(n)), say with certainty which pair is the **public** and which the **private** key, and **determine d via the EEA** (the table with rᵢ, qᵢ₋₁, sᵢ, tᵢ down to remainder 0). For large exponents, **square-and-multiply** is part of it (binary exponent from the left, bit 1 = square and multiply, bit 0 = square only, mod n after each step). Newly added for practice are the **signatures**: creating an **RSA signature** (s = mᵈ mod n) and verifying it (sᵉ mod n = m), as well as a **hash signature** (first H(m), then sign) — in the exercise sheet with the digit sum of the letter values as a toy hash. On the conceptual side: explain why **factorization** carries the security, what **Euler's φ** has to do with all of this, and why **textbook RSA** (deterministic, ×2 manipulation) needs **padding**. Factual knowledge: RSA was proposed in 1977 by Rivest, Shamir and Adleman, is based on the factorization problem, and for 128-bit symmetric security it needs about 3072 bits of RSA key length.

## More on this

- **Practical Networking — RSA Algorithm, with example** (EN): complete key generation and en-/decryption worked through on numbers. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pq8gNbvfaoM
- **Eddie Woo — The RSA Encryption Algorithm (1 of 2: Computing an Example)** (EN): very calm, small-step example computation. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4zahvcJ9glg
- **Art of the Problem — RSA Encryption** (EN): the intuition (trapdoor one-way function, Euler) behind RSA. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wXB-V_Keiu8
- **Square-and-Multiply — worked example** (EN): the algorithm step by step on a concrete power. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cbGB__V8MNk
expl:cs-2025-l06:content:
Until now everything revolved around keeping things secret — around confidentiality. This chapter is about the other great half of security, the half you need just as much in everyday life and yet often forget: *Who really sent this, and did it stay unchanged along the way?* These are the protection goals **authenticity** and **integrity**. We build three tools for this, and they are closely connected: the **digital signature** (the asymmetric, forgery-proof signature), the **hash function** (the digital fingerprint that makes signatures practical in the first place — and protects passwords on the side) and the **MAC** (the fast, symmetric cousin of the signature). By the end you will also understand why every reputable website carries a padlock symbol and why your password should never sit in plaintext in a database.

## Digital signatures: signing with the private key

Recall a weakness of symmetric cryptography: it cannot guarantee **non-repudiation**. Because both sides hold the same secret key, in a dispute it can never be proven *which* of them produced a message — both could have done it. The digital signature solves exactly this, and it does so simply by mirroring the RSA logic. When encrypting, you use the *public* key of the recipient; when signing, Alice uses her *own private* key. So she produces s = sig(x) with her private key and sends the pair (x, s). Anyone who knows her public key can then verify whether the signature fits — the result is simply true or false. Because only Alice holds her private key, only she can produce a valid signature; and because anyone with the public key can check it, the signature is publicly verifiable. This delivers three things at once: **integrity** (a message altered afterwards fails the check), **authenticity** (the message demonstrably comes from Alice) and thus the longed-for **non-repudiation**. This works with any asymmetric method — RSA, Diffie-Hellman, elliptic curves — and you already computed the concrete RSA variant by hand in the previous chapter.

![Digital signature: signing with the private key, verifying with the public one](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/99/Digital_Signature_diagram_de.svg "The digital signature is the mirror image of encryption: you sign with the private key, you check with the public one.")

> **Memory aid (mirror image):** **Encrypting: public → private.** **Signing: private → public.** With the *private* key you sign (only one person can do that), with the *public* one everyone checks.

## The active attacker: man-in-the-middle

So far Oskar was a *passive* eavesdropper who only listened in. Now a more dangerous adversary enters: the **active** attacker, who does not just read messages but intercepts and alters them. His classic trick is the **man-in-the-middle (MITM)**: he secretly positions himself between Alice and Bob and **swaps the public keys**. When Bob sends his public key to Alice, Oskar intercepts it and replaces it with *his own*. Alice now believes she has Bob's key, but in truth encrypts (or checks signatures) for Oskar — who reads everything, re-encrypts it and passes it on to Bob without the two noticing anything. This reveals an uncomfortable truth: a public key alone is useless as long as you don't know for sure *who* it belongs to.

## Certificates and PKI: who owns this key?

From this follows the fundamental problem of asymmetric cryptography: it no longer needs a *secret* channel to distribute public keys, but it does need an **authenticated** one — one on which you can trust a key's origin. The solution is **certificates**. A certificate firmly binds a public key to an identity, and this binding is digitally signed by a trusted third party, a **Certificate Authority (CA)**. A certificate for Alice looks, at its core, like this: it contains her public key and her identity, and over them the CA's signature, i.e. Cert_A = [ (kpub_A, ID_A), sig_CA(kpub_A, ID_A) ]. Whoever trusts the CA can check this signature and then knows for sure that the key really belongs to Alice. The clever part in everyday life: the public keys of the major CAs are already **pre-installed** in your browser and operating system, which is why your browser can automatically check every website certificate — hence the padlock symbol. The whole organisational apparatus around it (CAs, identity verification, revoking compromised certificates, securely distributing the CA keys) is called a **public-key infrastructure (PKI)**, and the standard format of a certificate is **X.509** with fields like serial number, issuer, validity period, subject, public key and the signature.

## Hash functions: the digital fingerprint

Now to perhaps the most versatile tool in all of cryptography. A **hash function h** takes an arbitrarily long input and turns it into a **fixed-length fingerprint** (typically 128 to 512 bits). Unlike a cipher it has **no key** — anyone can compute the same hash. Two properties make it useful: it is extremely fast even for very long messages, and it exhibits the **avalanche effect** — the smallest change to the input, a single flipped bit, changes the hash completely and unpredictably.

![A cryptographic hash function maps arbitrary inputs to a fixed-length fingerprint](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/2b/Cryptographic_Hash_Function.svg "A hash function compresses any input — no matter how long — into a fixed-length fingerprint. Even a tiny change to the input yields a completely different hash.")

What is this needed for with signatures? A signature can only ever sign a message of limited length (about 2048 bits with RSA-2048). Large documents cannot be signed directly, and signing them block by block as with a block cipher would be far too expensive. The elegant solution: **hash first, then sign the hash.** Alice computes z = h(x), signs only this short fingerprint and sends (x, s). Bob re-hashes the received x himself to z′ = h(x) and checks the signature against z′ — if they match, the document is unchanged and from Alice. This way you sign an arbitrarily large document with a short signature.

## The three security properties — and the birthday paradox

For this hash-then-sign to be secure, h must satisfy three properties that you must keep cleanly apart. The first is **preimage resistance**: from a given hash value h(x) you must not be able to compute back the input value x. Were this violated, an attacker could recover the original plaintext from a signed hash without breaking the encryption. The second is **weak collision resistance** (also second-preimage resistance): for a *given* message x₁ you must not be able to find a *different* message x₂ ≠ x₁ that has the same hash. Were this violated, an active attacker could secretly swap the signed message x₁ for his x₂ — the old signature over h(x₁) = h(x₂) would remain valid. The third and strictest is **strong collision resistance**: you must not be able to find *any* pair x₁ ≠ x₂ with the same hash at all (whichever it is).

Here comes a punchline that surprises almost everyone. Collisions *must* exist — there are infinitely many possible inputs but only finitely many hash values (pigeonhole principle). The only question is *how hard* it is to find one. The answer is given by the **birthday paradox**: in a group of just 23 people, the probability that two share a birthday is already over 50 % — far more than intuition expects, because what matters is the number of *pairs*, not of people. Transferred to an n-bit hash this means: for a collision you need not 2ⁿ attempts but only about **2^(n/2)**. So a 256-bit hash offers only about 128 bits of collision security — which is why hashes are chosen generously long. The most important hash functions come from two camps: the **MD4 family** (to which MD5, SHA-1 and SHA-2 belong; they compute on 32-bit values with boolean operations like AND, OR, XOR) and hashes based on block ciphers; **SHA-3** deliberately does *not* belong to the MD4 family, because it is built differently internally. SHA-1, for instance, processes the message in 512-bit blocks via a compression function with padding and yields 160 bits — the typical Merkle-Damgård construction:

![Merkle-Damgård construction: the message is hashed block by block through a compression function](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/ed/Merkle-Damgard_hash_big.svg "The Merkle-Damgård construction (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2): the padded message runs block by block through a compression function, the result flowing into the next block each time.")

> **Memory aid (birthday paradox):** A collision costs only *half* the bit count in the exponent — **2^(n/2)**, not 2ⁿ. A hash is therefore „twice as long" as the security it delivers.

> **Memory aid (three properties):** **Preimage** = can't get from h(x) back to x. **Weak collision** = for a *given* x, can't find a second one. **Strong collision** = find *any* pair with the same hash (the easiest — hence the birthday paradox).

### Step by step: estimating collision probability

This is exactly what the exercise sheet has you compute, and the result astonishes every time. For N possible hash values and k randomly inserted values, you approximate the collision probability with **P ≈ 1 − e^(−k(k−1)/2N)**. Take a **48-bit address space**, i.e. N = 2⁴⁸ ≈ 2.8·10¹⁴, and **k = 2.3·10⁷** files:

- The numerator in the exponent is k(k−1)/2 ≈ k²/2 = (2.3·10⁷)² / 2 ≈ 2.6·10¹⁴.
- The exponent is therefore −2.6·10¹⁴ / 2.8·10¹⁴ ≈ −0.94.
- Thus P ≈ 1 − e^(−0.94) ≈ 1 − 0.39 ≈ **0.61**, i.e. about **60 %**.

Over 60 % collision probability with „only" 23 million entries in a 281-trillion-sized space — that is the force of the birthday paradox, and exactly why one prefers hashes of 256 rather than 128 bits.

### Step by step: filling a hash table

Hashes also have a thoroughly non-cryptographic application that the exercise sheet picks up: the **hash table**. Here the hash serves to quickly assign each entry to one of several containers (buckets). With six buckets the hash function is simply H(k) = k mod 6, and on a collision you append the new value to a list in the same bucket (separate chaining). Inserting 71, 36, 22, 38, 11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 112, 42 in order: 71 mod 6 = 5, 36 mod 6 = 0, 22 mod 6 = 4, 38 mod 6 = 2, 11 mod 6 = 5 (collision, behind 71), 10 mod 6 = 4 (behind 22), 1 mod 6 = 1, 6 mod 6 = 0 (behind 36), 4 mod 6 = 4, 112 mod 6 = 4, 42 mod 6 = 0. This yields the buckets 0: {36, 6, 42}, 1: {1}, 2: {38}, 3: {}, 4: {22, 10, 4, 112}, 5: {71, 11}.

![A hash table with separate chaining for collision resolution](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/7d/Hash_table_3_1_1_0_1_0_0_SP.svg "Hash table with separate chaining: colliding entries land in the same bucket and are chained into a list.")

If you insert the same numbers in a *different order*, the important observation emerges: which number lands in which bucket depends solely on the hash value, *not* on the order — so the bucket membership is identical. All that differs is the *order within* a chain. That is exactly the point of the task.

## Storing passwords correctly: hashing, rainbow tables and salting

Perhaps the most everyday application of hashes concerns passwords. If a website stores usernames and passwords **in plaintext**, a single data leak is catastrophic: the attacker immediately has all login credentials — and because many people reuse passwords, those for other services as well. That is why one never stores the password itself, only its **hash**. At login the server hashes the input and compares it with the stored hash; thanks to preimage resistance even an attacker with the whole database cannot compute the passwords back.

But this is not yet fully watertight, because attackers come with a **rainbow table attack**: huge, precomputed tables that already contain the hash for millions of common passwords. If a password hash is in such a table, the password is instantly exposed. The countermeasure is called **salting**: before hashing, you append to each password a random value that differs per user (the salt) and store it alongside. As a result, even the same password produces completely different hashes for two users, and a precomputed rainbow table becomes useless — the attacker would have to recompute it for every single salt.

## MAC and HMAC: integrity with a shared key

Sometimes you want to ensure integrity and authenticity but need no non-repudiation and would like more speed. For this there is the **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** — a cryptographic checksum. Like a signature it guarantees that a message is genuine and unchanged; but unlike a signature it rests on **symmetric** cryptography with a shared key k_AB, is thereby considerably **faster** — and offers precisely **no non-repudiation**, because both sides know the same key and so either of them could have produced the MAC.

The obvious idea would be to build a MAC from a hash function by hashing the key together with the message. But two simple approaches have weaknesses. With the **secret-prefix MAC** h(k‖x) and the **secret-suffix MAC** h(x‖k) an attack can be found in each case — with the secret-suffix one, for instance, Oskar exploits a hash collision: if he finds an x_O with h(x) = h(x_O), he can swap x for x_O, and the checksum h(x‖k) = h(x_O‖k) stays valid. The robust standard solution is **HMAC** (1996, by Bellare, Canetti and Krawczyk): it hashes the key in two nested rounds with two fixed masks (ipad and opad) and is thereby secure even if the underlying hash function later shows weaknesses. HMAC is found today in TLS and IPSec; the construction reads HMAC(x) = h( (k⁺ ⊕ opad) ‖ h( (k⁺ ⊕ ipad) ‖ x ) ).

![The HMAC construction: two nested hash passes with a padded key](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/7f/SHAhmac.svg "HMAC: the padded key is masked with ipad and opad respectively and combined with the message in two nested hash passes.")

> **Memory aid (MAC vs. signature):** Both ensure integrity and authenticity. But **MAC = symmetric, fast, no non-repudiation**; **signature = asymmetric, slower, with non-repudiation**. Whoever must be able to argue *who* it was needs a signature.

## In a nutshell

The short version: this chapter is about integrity and authenticity rather than secrecy. A **digital signature** is the mirror image of encryption — you sign with the private key and verify with the public one, which delivers integrity, authenticity and non-repudiation. So that no **man-in-the-middle** slips in false public keys, a **certificate** signed by a **CA** binds the key to an identity (PKI, X.509). **Hash functions** are keyless fixed-length fingerprints with an avalanche effect; you always sign the hash, not the whole message. Their three goals are preimage, weak and strong collision resistance — and because of the **birthday paradox**, a collision needs only ~2^(n/2) instead of 2ⁿ attempts. Hashes also protect **passwords** (with **salting** against rainbow tables). The **MAC** is the symmetric, faster counterpart to the signature without non-repudiation; the secure standard is **HMAC**.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **sig_kpr(x) / Verify_kpub(s, x)** | signing with the private / checking with the public key |
| **Non-repudiation** | cannot be denied afterwards — only asymmetric (signature) |
| **MITM** | man-in-the-middle: active attacker swaps keys |
| **Certificate / CA** | signed binding key↔identity / issuing authority |
| **PKI / X.509** | public-key infrastructure / standard certificate format |
| **Hash h(x)** | keyless fixed-length fingerprint |
| **Avalanche effect** | smallest input change → completely different hash |
| **Preimage / collision resistance** | the three security goals of a hash function |
| **Birthday paradox** | collision already at ~2^(n/2) attempts |
| **Salting** | random value per password against rainbow tables |
| **MAC / HMAC** | symmetric checksum / secure hash-based MAC |

## Typical pitfalls

- **Signing = encrypting with the private key?** Conceptually reversed: **sign with private, check with public** — encryption is with public, decryption with private.
- **A hash protects confidentiality? No** — it is keyless and irreversible and ensures **integrity**, not secrecy.
- **An n-bit hash offers 2ⁿ collision security? No** — because of the birthday paradox only **2^(n/2)**.
- **A MAC delivers non-repudiation? No** — only a signature can. A MAC ensures only integrity and authenticity.
- **The secret-prefix/suffix MAC is secure? No** — both have known weaknesses; use HMAC.
- **Hashed passwords are automatically safe? Not without a salt** — otherwise a rainbow table cracks the common ones.

## Exam focus

The exercise sheet sets two practical priorities. First **hashing and the birthday paradox**: computing the **collision probability** with the approximation 1 − e^(−k(k−1)/2N) (e.g. „how likely is a collision with 2.3·10⁷ files and a 48-bit address space?"), explaining in general why ~2^(n/2) attempts already suffice, and filling a **hash table** with H(k) = k mod m via separate chaining (including the observation that the insertion order changes only the chain order, not the bucket membership). Second **password hashing**: explaining why you never store passwords in plaintext, what a **rainbow table attack** is and how **salting** prevents it. On the knowledge side: explaining the **signature procedure** (sign with kpr, check with kpub) and distinguishing it from encryption, describing the **MITM on the key exchange** and how **certificates/CA/PKI** prevent it, why you **hash first and then sign**, the **three hash properties** each with an attack scenario, and **MAC versus signature** (symmetric/asymmetric, speed, non-repudiation) including the weakness of the secret-suffix MAC and why **HMAC** fixes it.

## More on this

- **Computerphile — Hashing Algorithms and Security** (EN): the three requirements for hashes, the avalanche effect and why collisions are dangerous. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=b4b8ktEV4Bg
- **Practical Networking — Hashing, Algorithms, and Collisions** (EN): digest, collisions and the SHA family explained cleanly. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HHQ2QP_upGM
- **SHA-256 live demo** (interactive): type text and watch how the hash changes completely with every change. https://andersbrownworth.com/blockchain/hash
expl:cs-2025-l07:content:
Bitcoin is the great application in which all the crypto building blocks so far converge: hash functions and digital signatures are wired together here into a monetary system **without a bank**. The one question around which everything revolves is: how do you prevent someone from spending the same money twice without a central trusted authority — and who is even allowed to create new money? The answers are called blockchain, distributed consensus and proof-of-work, and they consist entirely of things you already got to know in the last chapters. We build Bitcoin from the ground up, building block by building block, until in the end it is clear why you can entrust your money to this unfamiliar machine.

## The basic problem: digital money without a bank

First picture what money is today. Classical book money — credit card, PayPal — is **centralized**: you trust a third party that keeps the books, and nothing about it is anonymous. Cash, on the other hand, is **decentralized**, anonymous and works offline, but has no digital twin. Bitcoin wants to carry over the best of both: a **decentralized peer-to-peer network**, but only **pseudonymous** (not perfectly anonymous, since all transactions are public) and as a rule online. From this arise three research problems: true **decentralization**, a sensible **anonymity** and above all the **double-spending problem** — because a digital coin is basically just a file, and files can be copied at will. How do you prevent someone from spending the same coin twice when no bank stands in between? Clever minds had long grappled with this problem (the Byzantine generals problem 1980, Chaum's Ecash 1989, Haber and Stornetta's timestamp log 1991, Merkle's hash tree 1979) before Bitcoin put the pieces together.

## Hash pointers and the blockchain

The first building block is a tiny but powerful trick. An ordinary **pointer** is a reference to a region of data. A **hash pointer** not only points there but additionally contains the **hash** of that region — so you can check at any time whether the data has been altered since. If you chain data blocks together so that each block contains the hash pointer to its predecessor, a **blockchain** arises: basically a linked list in which every „prev" pointer is a hash pointer. This makes the chain a **tamper-proof log**.

![A blockchain: each block references the previous one via a hash pointer](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/98/Blockchain.svg "Blockchain: each block contains the hash of the previous block. If you change an old block, all subsequent hashes break.")

The clever part is that the hash computation of a block covers **all** of its data — the transactions *and* the prev-hash of the predecessor. If the attacker Eve wants to forge an old transaction in block 10, its hash changes as a result; then the prev entry in block 11 no longer matches, so its hash changes too, and so on to the end of the chain. Eve would therefore have to **recompute all subsequent blocks** to cover up the forgery. As long as the topmost, newest hash is securely known somewhere, any manipulation is immediately noticeable. In this way a chain of arbitrary length can be formed, reaching back to the very first block, the **genesis block**.

## Merkle trees

A pure chain, however, has a drawback: if you want to prove that a particular transaction is included, you would in the worst case have to go through everything. This is where Ralph Merkle's 1979 invention comes into play, the **hash tree** (Merkle tree): a binary tree of hash pointers. You hash two neighboring data leaves together, then two of those results, and so on, until at the very top a single root remains — the **Merkle root**, which condenses all the data below it into a single value.

![Merkle tree: data leaves are hashed up in pairs to the Merkle root](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/95/Hash_Tree.svg "Merkle tree: each data leaf is hashed, two hashes at a time are hashed together further — at the very top the Merkle root, which condenses all data.")

The big advantage over the pure chain: you can **efficiently prove that an element is included** (a proof of membership) without checking all the data. The path from the root to the leaf in question suffices — so for n leaves only about log(n) hashes instead of all n.

> **Memory aid (Merkle tree):** hash leaves up in pairs to *one* root. To prove that a datum is in there, you need only **log(n)** hashes along the path — not all the data.

## Identities and Bitcoin addresses

In a system without a bank, who actually is „you"? In Bitcoin, **public keys are the identities**. This works because a correctly signed message can only come from the one who owns the matching private key — the signature *is* the proof of identity. From this follows a **decentralized identity management**: anyone can create as many identities as they like simply by generating new key pairs; no one has to approve it. Because public keys, however, are long and unwieldy, you derive a compact **Bitcoin address** from them (comparable to an IBAN), namely via the chain public key → SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 → prefix 00 → Base58Check → address.

## Distributed consensus and the double spend

Now to the heart of it: how do thousands of unfamiliar nodes agree on *one* common truth without a boss? Every node owns the blockchain on which consensus already exists, plus a pool of still-pending transactions. The simplified consensus algorithm runs like this: new transactions are broadcast to all nodes; each node collects them into a block; in each round an initially *randomly* chosen node shares its block (that is the simplification we will lift in a moment); the others accept it only if all contained transactions are valid (the coins not yet spent, the signatures correct); and they show their acceptance by including the hash of this block in their own next block.

Where is the attack? In a **double spend**, Alice sends her 100 coins simultaneously to Bob *and* to herself (to a second address of her own, Alice'). Both transactions are validly signed, so **two competing branches** of the chain arise. Which one wins? The rule is called **longest chain wins**: nodes keep building on the branch they saw first, and in the long run one of the two becomes longer — the shorter one becomes obsolete, and with it the transaction contained in it. In practice this means: if Bob waits for no confirmation at all (a zero-confirmation transaction), the attack is easy; the more confirmations he waits for, the safer his money is. The rule of thumb is: **after 6 confirmations** the double-spend chance is practically zero.

> **Memory aid (double spend):** two contradictory payments → two chain branches → **the longest branch wins**. Wait for **6 confirmations**, then the double spend is as good as impossible. [3Blue1Brown builds](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bBC-nXj3Ng4) the whole construction from the ground up.

## Proof-of-work and the incentives

The „random node choice" from the simplified algorithm is not only unrealistic, it is also attackable: anyone who can create arbitrarily many identities can also create arbitrarily many nodes and seize the choice (a Sybil attack). Bitcoin therefore replaces the random choice with **proof-of-work** — a competition for a scarce, unforgeable resource: **computing power**. The idea comes from **Hashcash**, which was originally meant to make spam harder. To be allowed to create a block, a miner must find a number, the **nonce**, such that the hash of the block lies below a threshold: **H(nonce ‖ prev ‖ transactions) < target**. The puzzle is hard to solve but easy for anyone to check, and the **target** sets the difficulty (the smaller it is, the more leading zeros the hash must have).

![Structure of a Bitcoin block with nonce and proof-of-work](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/55/Bitcoin_Block_Data.svg "A Bitcoin block: predecessor hash, Merkle root of the transactions and the nonce, which the miner varies until the block hash lies below the target.")

Because mining costs real energy, honest nodes must be rewarded — Bitcoin assumes here that more than half of the computing power is in honest hands. The reward has two sources. The first is the **block reward**: every block contains a special **coinbase transaction** that pays out fresh coins to the miner. This reward halves every 210,000 blocks (it began at 50 BTC and dropped via 25 in 2012, 12.5 (2016), 6.25 (2020) to 3.125 (2024)); around 2140 it reaches zero. The second source is the **transaction fees**: if you choose the output of a transaction smaller than the input, the difference goes to the miner — this mechanism keeps miners motivated even once there is no longer any block reward. How a node even recognizes whether coins have already been spent is governed by the **UTXO model** (unspent transaction output): a transaction consists of inputs (each an address plus an unspent earlier transaction, signed) and outputs (address plus amount), and the difference is the fee.

> **Memory aid (proof-of-work):** try out nonces until the block hash is **small enough** (below the target = begins with enough zeros). **Searching is expensive, checking is cheap.** You can try out exactly this dice-rolling for a valid nonce yourself in the [ETH.BUILD demo](https://ethereum.org/videos/blockchain-eth-build/).

### Step by step: mining a block

In the exercise sheet a simplified **mining task** appears, and the procedure is always the same. You are given a hash formula and the fixed fields of a block (the prev-hash, the time, the transaction root) and are to **find the nonce with which the validity condition is met** — say „block hash ≥ 90" with the toy currency CyberCoin or in general „hash < target". You simply plug nonce = 0 into the formula, compute the hash and check the condition; if it is not met, you take nonce = 1, then 2, and so on, until it fits. That is exactly what real miners do — only a billion times per second with real SHA-256 hashes. And the trick is „hard to find, easy to check": once a miner has found the right nonce, anyone else can recompute the hash *a single time* and immediately confirm its validity.

### Step by step: deriving the maximum coin supply

The second favorite task is the **maximum coin supply from the halving**, and it is a nice geometric series. With Bitcoin the reward starts at 50 BTC and halves every 210,000 blocks, so 50, then 25, then 12.5, and so on. The total supply is thus 210,000 · (50 + 25 + 12.5 + …) = 210,000 · 50 · (1 + ½ + ¼ + …). The series in the parentheses sums to **2**, so 210,000 · 50 · 2 = **21,000,000** comes out — the famous 21 million bitcoin. The same calculation works for any toy currency: if CyberCoin, say, has a reward of 10 that halves every 730 blocks, then there are at most 730 · 10 · 2 = **14,600** CyberCoins.

### Step by step: when a rainbow table is worthwhile

The same exercise sheet ties back once more to the password hashing from the previous chapter and has you work out *why* long passwords defeat rainbow tables. If there are 77 usable characters and password lengths up to 8 are allowed, then the number of possible passwords is 77⁴ + 77⁵ + … + 77⁸ — and this value is dominated by the last term, so roughly 77⁸ ≈ 1.2 · 10¹⁵. If you store 26 bytes per entry, the rainbow table would need about 26 · 1.2 · 10¹⁵ ≈ 3 · 10¹⁶ bytes, so roughly **30 petabytes**. And generating it at 200,000 hashes per second would take 1.2 · 10¹⁵ / 2 · 10⁵ ≈ 6 · 10⁹ seconds, so almost **200 years**. Exactly these numbers show why you choose passwords long (and add salt, see previous chapter): the precomputed table simply becomes unaffordable.

## In a nutshell

The short version: Bitcoin is decentralized, pseudonymous money without a bank, whose core problems are double-spending, consensus without a trusted authority, and the question of money creation. It is solved from four building blocks of the previous weeks: **hash pointers** chain blocks into a tamper-proof log (the **blockchain**) — if you change an old block, all subsequent hashes break; a **Merkle tree** condenses all transactions of a block into one root and allows efficient membership proofs; **public keys** serve as identities, from which the Bitcoin address is derived; and consensus arises through **proof-of-work**, in which miners search for a nonce with H(block) < target. „Longest chain wins" holds, after about 6 confirmations double-spending is practically impossible, and the incentives are supplied by the **block reward** (halves every 210,000 blocks, upper bound 21 million BTC) plus the **transaction fees**.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Double-spending** | spending the same coin twice |
| **Hash pointer** | pointer plus hash of the target region (tamper-proof) |
| **Blockchain** | blocks chained via hash pointers; start = genesis block |
| **Merkle tree / root** | hash tree; the root condenses all data |
| **Bitcoin address** | from the public key: SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 → Base58Check |
| **Proof-of-work** | find a nonce with H(nonce‖prev‖tx) < target |
| **Nonce** | the sought number that makes the block valid |
| **Longest chain wins** | the longest branch prevails |
| **Block reward / coinbase** | fresh coins to the miner, halves every 210,000 blocks |
| **UTXO** | unspent transaction output — available balance |

## Common pitfalls

- **Bitcoin is anonymous? No** — only **pseudonymous**; addresses instead of names, but all transactions are publicly traceable.
- **A transaction is immediately safe? No** — only after several **confirmations** (rule of thumb 6); with zero-confirmation the double spend looms.
- **The block hash covers only the transactions? No** — it also includes the **prev-hash**, which is why every manipulation propagates forward.
- **Proof-of-work „computes something useful"? No** — its sole purpose is that the work is **scarce and expensive**; only the nonce search counts.
- **The block reward stays constant? No** — it **halves** every 210,000 blocks, with the upper bound of 21 million BTC.

## Exam focus

The exercise sheet mixes theory with neat calculation problems. The free-explanation part includes the **structure and purpose of a blockchain**, the **Byzantine generals problem** in the Bitcoin context (agreeing on one truth without a central authority), the **proof-of-work** concept and the role of the **Merkle tree**. The typical calculation problem is a simplified **mining simulation**: with a given hash formula you are to **find the nonce with which the block hash meets the validity condition** (say hash ≥ threshold) for given block fields and thus complete a block table. In addition you should be able to compute the **maximum coin supply from a halving rule** via the geometric series (reward · blocks-per-halving · 2). And fitting the password hashing from the last chapter: estimate the **size and generation time of a rainbow table** (number of passwords via the combinatorics, times bytes per row, divided by the hash rate). More conceptual are the **manipulation scenario** of the blockchain, the **double spend** with „longest chain wins" and the 6-confirmations rule, as well as **block reward**, **transaction fees** and **UTXO** in broad strokes.

## Further reading

- **3Blue1Brown — But how does bitcoin actually work?** (~26 min, EN): builds Bitcoin step by step from ledger, signatures, hashes and proof-of-work — the best intuition on the subject. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bBC-nXj3Ng4
- **ETH.BUILD — blockchain mining demo** (interactive, EN): see live how blocks are chained, how proof-of-work secures them and what happens on manipulation. https://ethereum.org/videos/blockchain-eth-build/
- **Khan Academy — Bitcoin: Cryptographic hash functions** (EN): the hash foundation behind Bitcoin. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0WiTaBI82Mc
expl:cs-2025-l08:content:
We have ciphers now — symmetric as well as asymmetric — and digital signatures. Yet one old gap is still open, the same one that already plagued symmetric cryptography: *How do two strangers agree on a shared secret key over a wiretapped line without having exchanged it beforehand?* This chapter gives two fundamentally different answers. The first is a small miracle of mathematics — the **Diffie-Hellman key exchange**, in which Alice and Bob generate a shared key that *never* travels over the line. The second is more down-to-earth and server-based — protocols like **Needham-Schroeder** and **Kerberos**, which employ a trusted key server. Both have their pitfalls, and those are exactly what make this chapter exam-relevant.

## Diffie-Hellman: negotiating a secret in public

In 1976, Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman presented a method that at first glance seems impossible: two parties agree on a shared secret key by exchanging only *public* messages. This is made possible by a different one-way function than in RSA — not factorization, but the **discrete logarithm problem**: a power α^a mod p is easy to compute, but recovering the exponent a from the result is practically hopeless for large p. DHKE thereby solves the key distribution problem and today sits inside SSL/TLS and IPsec.

The nicest intuition for it is **mixing colors**: suppose that mixing two colors is easy, but decomposing a mixed color back into its components is practically impossible. Alice and Bob publicly agree on a shared base color. Then each secretly mixes in their own secret color and sends the result over openly. Now if each mixes the *received* mixture with their *own* secret color, both arrive at exactly the same final color — which an eavesdropper cannot reproduce, because they lack both secret colors.

![Diffie-Hellman as a color-mixing analogy](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/46/Diffie-Hellman_Key_Exchange.svg "Diffie-Hellman as colors: a public base color plus one secret color each yield, crosswise, the same final color — without revealing the secret colors.")

In numbers, it works like this. First the public **setup**: you choose a large prime p (ideally over 2048 bits) and a base α from the range 2 to p−2, and publish both. Then the **protocol**: Alice secretly chooses a number a, computes A = α^a mod p, and sends A to Bob; Bob secretly chooses b, computes B = α^b mod p, and sends B to Alice. Now Alice computes B^a = (α^b)^a = α^(a·b) mod p, and Bob computes A^b = (α^a)^b = α^(a·b) mod p — both obtain the same **session key k_AB = α^(a·b) mod p**, even though only A and B were ever transmitted. An eavesdropper Oskar knows α, p, A, and B, but would have to extract the secret exponent a from A = α^a — precisely the discrete logarithm — and that cannot be done for large p.

![Both sides compute the same shared secret](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4c/Public_key_shared_secret.svg "From the publicly exchanged values, both parties compute the same shared secret, which was never sent over the line.")

### Step by step: Diffie-Hellman by hand

Let's work it through with small numbers, exactly as on the exercise sheet. Let p = 13 and g = 2 be public (here g is the base α). Alice secretly chooses a = 4, Bob secretly chooses b = 5.

- Alice computes A = g^a mod p = 2⁴ mod 13 = 16 mod 13 = **3** and sends 3.
- Bob computes B = g^b mod p = 2⁵ mod 13 = 32 mod 13 = **6** and sends 6.
- Alice computes the secret s = B^a mod p = 6⁴ mod 13 = 1296 mod 13 = **9**.
- Bob computes s = A^b mod p = 3⁵ mod 13 = 243 mod 13 = **9**.

Both have **s = 9** — and only the 3 and the 6 went over the line, never the 9. (For larger exponents you again compute the powers with square-and-multiply and reduce modulo p after each step.) The one caveat you must never forget is this: without authentication, DHKE too is vulnerable to an active **man-in-the-middle** — Oskar simply negotiates a separate key with Alice *and* with Bob and sits unnoticed in the middle. That is why DHKE needs certificates in practice.

> **Memory aid (Diffie-Hellman = mixing colors):** public base color (α, p) plus one *secret* color each (a, b). Each sends their mixture; mixing the other's mixture with your own secret color yields **the same** final color. Un-mixing (= the discrete log) is impossible. [Computerphile shows exactly this analogy](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NmM9HA2MQGI).

## Key servers (KDC): the symmetric route

There is also a completely different way to distribute session keys — purely **symmetric**, via a trusted server, the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**. The assumption here: every user already possesses a secret symmetric key shared with the KDC, installed in advance (for instance by the system administrator when handing over the laptop). When two users now want to communicate, the KDC generates a fresh **session key** (also called a session or ephemeral key) and distributes it to both in encrypted form. Why short-lived session keys make sense at all: the attacker has only a few ciphertexts available per key, would have to crack many different keys to read along over longer communication (think of satellite television or voice encryption), and the symmetric establishment is computationally fast.

## Two attacks every key protocol must fend off

Such server-based protocols must be armed against two classic attacks. The first is the **replay attack**: is the received session key even *current*? If a protocol does not check this, an attacker can replay old, intercepted messages and pose as the KDC while doing so. The countermeasure is called **freshness** and is guaranteed by nonces (fresh random values), timestamps, or counters. The second is the **key confirmation attack**: has the other side really *confirmed* the key? If it does not demonstrably do so, Oskar can swap one of the identities and thereby establish a key that he himself knows. The countermeasure is a **challenge-response procedure** and the fixed **inclusion of the identities** in the exchanged messages.

> **Memory aid (replay vs. confirmation):** **Replay** asks "is the message *fresh*?" → countermeasure **freshness** (nonce/timestamp). **Confirmation** asks "is it really the *right* counterparty talking?" → countermeasure **challenge-response**. Note also: **Needham-Schroeder = nonces** (with a gap), **Kerberos = timestamps** (which requires synchronized clocks).

## Needham-Schroeder and Kerberos

The **Needham-Schroeder protocol** from 1978 is one such KDC protocol. It fends off key confirmation attacks because the identities are transmitted encrypted and the final messages confirm via challenge-response that Alice actually possesses the session key. But it has a famous **weakness**: if the protocol breaks off after the second message, an attacker who has captured an *old* session key (and Alice's key) can simply send the third message — and Bob does not notice that the key is long outdated. Nonces alone do not cover this case; here you would need timestamps.

That is exactly what the successor does. **Kerberos** builds on Needham-Schroeder, but is more than mere key transport: it **authenticates users across the entire network**, was standardized in 1993 as RFC 1510, and today sits in every Active Directory environment of Windows and Linux. The decisive difference is the use of **timestamps** instead of mere nonces, which eliminates the replay attack just described — the price for this being **synchronized clocks** among all participants. Both protocols, however, share a fundamental assumption: a **trusted third party**, the KDC. And that is at the same time their weak point — a **single point of failure** whose compromise unhinges the entire system.

## In a nutshell

The short version: this chapter closes the gap "how do two strangers agree on a key over a wiretapped line?". The first answer is **Diffie-Hellman**: Alice and Bob *jointly* generate the key k_AB = α^(a·b) mod p without ever sending it — secure thanks to the discrete logarithm problem, but MITM-vulnerable without authentication (hence certificates). The second answer is **server-based protocols** with a KDC that distributes symmetric session keys (everyone shares a key with the KDC in advance). Such protocols must fend off two attacks: **replay** (countermeasure freshness via nonce/timestamp) and **missing key confirmation** (countermeasure challenge-response). **Needham-Schroeder** (1978) still has a replay gap; **Kerberos** closes it with timestamps (requiring synchronized clocks for that) and authenticates users on the network. Both presuppose a KDC — a single point of failure.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **DHKE** | Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange |
| **discrete logarithm problem** | the one-way function behind DHKE (and ECC) |
| **α, p (or g, p)** | public parameters: base/generator and large prime |
| **a, b** | the private exponents of Alice and Bob |
| **k_AB = α^(a·b) mod p** | the shared session key |
| **KDC** | Key Distribution Center — the key server |
| **session/ephemeral key** | short-lived session key |
| **replay attack** | replaying old messages |
| **freshness** | currency via nonce / timestamp / counter |
| **challenge-response** | proof that the counterparty has the key |
| **Needham-Schroeder / Kerberos** | KDC protocols (nonces / timestamps) |

## Common pitfalls

- **Is the key transmitted in DHKE? No** — only A and B are transmitted; the key α^(a·b) is *computed* by both, never sent.
- **Is DHKE secure against MITM? No** — without authentication/certificates it is vulnerable, like all asymmetric methods.
- **Do DHKE and RSA use the same problem? No** — DHKE rests on the **discrete logarithm**, RSA on **factorization**.
- **Do nonces solve every replay? Not quite** — the Needham-Schroeder gap shows that **timestamps** are needed here.
- **Does a KDC bring only advantages? No** — it is a trusted third party and therefore a **single point of failure**.

## Exam focus

By far the most important exercise of this chapter is the **Diffie-Hellman calculation** — practice it until you can do it blind: from p, g, a, b you compute A = g^a mod p, B = g^b mod p, and the shared secret s = B^a mod p = A^b mod p. The exercise sheet has you work this through on several parameter sets (for instance p = 13, g = 2, a = 4, b = 5 → s = 9), and that is exactly how it comes up in the exam. Alongside this, explain **why DHKE is secure** (discrete logarithm) and **why a MITM** nevertheless succeeds without authentication. The second focus is **certificates and TLS**: the scope of tasks of a **CA**, the approaches to **revoking** a certificate (the revocation list CRL and real-time **OCSP**), the difference between the **CA-based trust structure and the web of trust** (GPG), as well as the idea of **Let's Encrypt** and **Certificate Transparency**. On the protocol side: **replay** versus **key confirmation attack** with their respective countermeasures (freshness/nonce or challenge-response), the **Needham-Schroeder weakness** (break-off after message 2), and **Kerberos** (timestamps instead of nonces, synchronized clocks needed, KDC as single point of failure).

## Further reading

- **Computerphile — Secret Key Exchange (Diffie-Hellman)** (~9 min, EN): the famous color-mixing analogy — why a secret can be negotiated in public. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NmM9HA2MQGI
- **Computerphile — Public Key Cryptography** (~6 min, EN): placing asymmetric methods and one-way functions in context. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GSIDS_lvRv4
- **Wikipedia — Diffie-Hellman key exchange** (DE): protocol, worked example, and the MITM problem in detail. https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diffie-Hellman-Schl%C3%BCsselaustausch
