Here the course leaves pure mathematics behind and enters the world of network and web security. Two very different attackers appear. The first doesn't want to spy on your system at all, but simply to **bring it down** — he attacks availability (denial-of-service). The second smuggles **malicious input** into your web application and tricks it into executing his code (cross-site scripting and SQL injection). Before that, in a single sentence, stands the protocol that ties together all the cryptography of the past weeks on the web: TLS. By the end you'll understand why a single hijacked refrigerator can be part of an attack that takes half a continent off the grid — and why a single quotation mark in an input field can make a database spill its secrets.

## TLS in one sentence

**SSL/TLS** is the hybrid protocol that secures all „https" traffic: it uses asymmetric cryptography for the key exchange and certificate verification, and then switches to fast symmetric encryption for the actual data — exactly the combination we built up over the past chapters. Its version history is above all a long list of deprecations: SSL 1.0 through 3.0 and TLS 1.0/1.1 are considered obsolete today; only **TLS 1.2 (2008)** and **TLS 1.3 (2018)** are still relevant and secure.

## Denial-of-Service: attacking availability

A **denial-of-service attack (DoS)** targets the third CIA protection goal directly, **availability**. Its sole aim is to overload a resource so badly that legitimate requests can no longer be served — the vulnerable targets being network bandwidth, system resources, or application resources. There is a whole family of techniques for this, and you should be able to tell them apart. The simplest is **ICMP flooding**: the attacker showers the server with „ping" requests (ICMP echo requests) until it chokes on the load of responding. To stay undetected while doing so, he uses **source-address spoofing** — he forges the sender address of his packets, which not only disguises him but also lets him deliberately redirect the responses onto other people's machines. A more cunning variant is **SYN spoofing**, which abuses the TCP connection setup (the three-way handshake): the attacker opens huge numbers of half-open connections that the server keeps open until its connection table is full.

Two amplification tricks become truly dangerous. In a **reflection attack** the attacker sends requests with the victim's forged sender address to many trusting servers — their responses then all hit the victim, who is flooded with someone else's response traffic. In an **amplification attack** a second leverage effect is added: a *small* request triggers a *much larger* response. The textbook example is **DNS amplification**, in which a 60-byte request (which uses „ANY" to demand as much information as possible) produces a 512- to 4000-byte response — the attacker multiplies his own bandwidth many times over. And finally the **distributed DoS (DDoS)**: instead of coming from a single machine, the attack comes from thousands at once, a **botnet** of hijacked machines that an attacker remotely controls via a command-and-control server.

![How a botnet works: from the malware through the C&C server to the coordinated attack](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c6/Botnet.svg "A botnet: infected machines report to a command-and-control server, which then issues them coordinated attack commands.")

Exactly such a botnet was **Mirai** (2016), the textbook example par excellence: an **IoT botnet** that hijacked poorly secured everyday devices — surveillance cameras, video recorders, home routers — via their **weak default passwords** and open services (often Telnet), spread on its own to further vulnerable devices, and with them ran the largest DDoS attacks in history up to that point. The following architecture shows how such attacks fan out across handler and agent zombies:

![Stachledraht: a distributed DoS attack via handlers and agents](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3f/Stachledraht_DDos_Attack.svg "A DDoS attack: the attacker controls an army of agent zombies via handlers, which together flood the victim.")

The defense is hard for precisely this reason: you have to **distinguish legitimate from malicious load** — the attack traffic often looks like perfectly normal requests. The tools against it are more bandwidth and redundancy, attack detection and filtering (for instance by firewalls), and the attempt to identify the attacker.

> **Memory aid (DoS variants):** **Reflection** = *redirect* the response onto the victim (forged sender address). **Amplification** = *inflate* the response (small question, huge answer, e.g. DNS „ANY"). **DDoS** = *many* machines (botnet) instead of one. And remember: DoS always hits the **A** of the CIA triad.

## Input validation: XSS and SQL injection

The second half of the chapter is about a completely different mistake: what happens when a web application reuses **user input without checking it**? The common root cause of all the following attacks is always the same — data (the input) and code (the surrounding HTML or SQL) become mixed up, and the attacker smuggles code into a place where the program only expects data. (The examples are in PHP; you don't need to be able to write PHP code, but you should be able to read it and spot the flaw.)

In **cross-site scripting (XSS)** the page echoes an input directly into the HTML, for example a greeting „Welcome " followed by the parameter name. If the attacker enters, as name, not his name but a piece of JavaScript — say an **alert(document.cookie)** inside a script tag — then this script ends up in the delivered HTML and is **executed in the victim's browser**. With this the attacker can, for example, read out the victim's session cookies and take over their session. Three flavors are distinguished: **reflected XSS** (the script is in the link or request and is immediately reflected back), the more dangerous **stored XSS** (the script is stored on the server, for instance in a forum post, and hits everyone who visits the page afterward), and **DOM-based XSS** (which arises entirely in client-side JavaScript).

In **SQL injection** the input lands unchecked in a database query. Picture the typical login check: **SELECT uid FROM users WHERE username = '$user' AND password = '$pass'**. If the attacker enters **admin'--** as the username, this becomes **… WHERE username = 'admin'--' AND password = '…'** — the two hyphens start a comment in SQL that **comments out** the entire rest of the line (and with it the password check). What remains is a query that returns the admin account with no password at all: a classic **login bypass**. The input **' OR '1'='1** has the same effect, appending an always-true condition. And with a **UNION** injection (for example name = foo' UNION SELECT password FROM users) the meaning of the query can be bent so that it returns foreign data such as passwords.

> **Memory aid (XSS vs. SQLi):** Both arise because **data is read as code**. **XSS** smuggles in **JavaScript** → it runs in the *victim's browser* (steal cookies). **SQLi** smuggles in **database commands** → they run in the *database* (read data, change it, bypass login). The lone quotation mark in an input is the classic SQLi warning sign.

## Defense: sanitization, validation, whitelisting, CSP

The same principle helps against both attacks, **input validation**, in two flavors. The first is **sanitization and transformation** — you *reshape the input* before using it. This includes **typecasting** (forcibly coercing an input into a type such as integer, so that smuggled-in text has no effect at all), **encoding** (rendering special characters harmless), and **context-sensitive sanitization** (for instance a function that converts the dangerous characters < and > into their harmless HTML entities < and >, or a regular expression that only lets allowed characters through). The second flavor is **condition-based validation** — you *check the input* instead of reshaping it: whether it really is a number (type validation), whether it has a valid format (format validation), and above all whether it appears on a list of allowed values.

The most important maxim here: **whitelisting beats blacklisting.** A blacklist of forbidden inputs is always error-prone, because you inevitably overlook a dangerous case; a whitelist permits only what is explicitly known and is therefore fundamentally safer. As a complement there is the **Content Security Policy (CSP)**, a W3C standard that uses a whitelist to specify which sources a page may load content from, and which by default blocks inline scripts as well as dangerous functions like eval. But beware the classic misconception: **CSP does not replace input validation**, it is only an additional layer of protection — defense in depth, not a cure-all.

## In a nutshell

The short version: here the course leaves mathematics. **TLS** ties cryptography together on the web in hybrid fashion (asymmetric key exchange, then symmetric data). Two attacker types are at the center. One wants to bring things down: **denial-of-service** attacks **availability** (the A of the CIA) by overloading resources — via ICMP flooding, source-address spoofing, SYN spoofing, **reflection** (redirecting responses onto the victim), and **amplification** (small request, huge response), and distributed as a **DDoS** across a botnet like **Mirai**. The other smuggles malicious input into web applications: **XSS** (injected JavaScript runs in the victim's browser) and **SQL injection** (injected database commands, all the way to a login bypass with admin'-- or ' OR '1'='1). Both are defended against with **input validation** — sanitization and encoding, type and format checking, **whitelisting** (better than blacklisting) — and complemented by **CSP**, which however does not replace input checking.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **TLS** | hybrid protocol for the web (asym. key exchange + sym. data) |
| **DoS / DDoS** | attack availability / distributed across a botnet |
| **ICMP flooding** | overload the server with ping requests |
| **source-address spoofing** | forge the source address |
| **reflection / amplification** | steer responses onto the victim / small request → big response |
| **botnet / Mirai** | remotely controlled hijacked machines / IoT botnet 2016 |
| **XSS** | injected JavaScript in the victim's browser |
| **SQL injection** | injected database commands (login bypass, data theft) |
| **sanitization / validation** | reshape input / check input |
| **whitelisting** | allow only what is explicitly permitted (better than blacklisting) |
| **CSP** | Content Security Policy (complements input validation) |

## Common pitfalls

- **DoS violates confidentiality? No** — DoS attacks **availability** (the A of the CIA triad).
- **Reflection is the same as amplification? Not quite** — reflection steers responses onto the victim; **amplification** additionally magnifies them (small request → big response).
- **DDoS is just one especially powerful machine? No** — it's **many** machines (a botnet); that is exactly what makes it so hard to defend against.
- **Blacklisting is enough as a defense? No** — it is error-prone; **whitelisting** is safer.
- **CSP replaces input validation? No** — it is only an additional layer (defense in depth).

## Exam focus

The exercise sheet is very web-heavy and concrete. For **XSS** you should be able to distinguish the **three types** — **reflected** (the script is in the request and is immediately reflected back), **stored** (the script is stored on the server and hits every visitor), and **DOM-based** (purely in client-side JavaScript) — and explain **CSP** with its pros and cons. For **SQL injection** you must be able to **formulate a login bypass concretely** (for example the username admin'-- or ' OR '1'='1, which comments out or defeats the password check) and say how to **prevent** it (parameterized queries or prepared statements and sanitization). For the **DoS** part: name the technical goal, assign it to **CIA availability** (with justification), describe the **DDoS techniques** and the **amplification** concept with examples, and distinguish **DoS from DDoS** (why distributed is harder to defend against). For the **botnets**: name well-known ones, describe infection paths, explain what a **command-and-control server** is, and **sketch** a botnet (attacker → C&C → bots → victim) with the DDoS communication flows. And on **defense**: the detection problems at layer 7 of the OSI model, the capacity dilemma, the difference between on-site and off-site measures, and how a **CDN** with DNS-based routing cushions a DDoS.

## Further reading

- **Computerphile — Hacking Websites with SQL Injection** (EN): Tom Scott vividly shows how unchecked input alters SQL commands. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_jKylhJtPmI
- **PwnFunction — Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Explained** (~12 min., EN): how injected JavaScript ends up in the victim's browser. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EoaDgUgS6QA
- **Aikido — Injection Attacks 101 (SQLi, Code Injection, XSS)** (EN): a compact overview including defense through input validation. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wu6FAsiFhv0

This lecture shows how a harmless programming mistake in C turns into a complete takeover of the machine — and how the eternal arms race between attack and defense plays out along the way. The through-line: a memory error allows the return address to be overwritten; depending on which defense is active, the attacker either injects their own code (code injection) or assembles the attack out of existing code (code reuse / ROP). Against this stand DEP, ASLR, and CFI.

## Why C programs are so dangerous

C and C++ produce fast native code — but they perform **no array-bounds checks**. Writing into char buffer[8] at position buffer[8] (or worse: at an index computed from runtime data) is **undefined behavior**: at best a crash (segmentation fault), at worst **arbitrary code execution**. Classic pitfalls are unsafe string functions like strcpy or gets — the latter reads arbitrarily much input into a fixed buffer **without any length check**. That is exactly the point of entry. Such memory errors have been "Public Enemy No. 1" since the Morris worm (1988) — and drive a decades-long arms race (code injection → return-into-libc 1997 → ROP 2007).

## x86 basics: stack, registers, stack frame

To understand the attack, you need a bit of x86 knowledge:

- **Process layout:** code (.text), data (.data/.bss), the **heap** (grows upward), and the **stack** (grows **downward**, toward low addresses).
- **Registers:** general-purpose registers (EAX, EBX, …), the **stack pointer ESP** (points to the topmost stack element), the **base pointer EBP** (references arguments/local variables), and the **instruction pointer EIP** — it points to the *next* instruction and can only be changed via jump instructions (CALL, JMP, RET). (x86_64: RAX…RIP, plus R8–R15.)
- **Stack frame:** every function call lays down a frame, from top to bottom: **function arguments**, **return address**, **saved base pointer**, **local variables** (including our buffers!).
- **Calling convention:** call automatically places the **return address** on the stack; ret retrieves it and loads it into **EIP**. It is exactly this saved return address that is the target.

![The call stack with multiple stack frames](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8a/ProgramCallStack2_en.png "The call stack: each function call lays down a new frame — with arguments, return address, saved base pointer, and local variables.")

![Structure of a single stack frame](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1f/Call-stack-layout.svg "A single stack frame in detail: arguments, return address, saved base pointer, and local variables. The buffer sits among the local variables and, on overflow, runs toward the return address.")

> **Memory aid (stack frame, top to bottom):** **arguments → return address → old EBP → local variables** (including the buffer). The stack grows **downward**, but the buffer overflows **upward** — straight toward the **return address**. That is the whole secret of the attack.

## Code injection: hijacking the return

### Step by step

Example: an echo program reads with gets(buffer) into a local buffer and prints it out.

1. The attacker enters **more** data than the buffer can hold.
2. The overlength runs **upward** in the stack and overwrites the saved base pointer and finally the **return address**.
3. The attacker places their **shellcode** in the buffer (or behind it) and sets the return address to its beginning.
4. On ret the CPU does **not** jump back into main, but into the shellcode — which, for instance, opens a remote shell.

This adds a **completely new node** (the attacker's own code) to the control-flow graph — the attacker can do anything. [Computerphile demonstrates exactly this attack live](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1S0aBV-Waeo).

## DEP, return-into-libc, and ROP

**Countermeasure DEP (Data Execution Prevention):** memory is either **writable or executable, never both** (W^X). The injected shellcode lies in the data area (stack) — and that is not executable. Code injection fails.

**The attackers' answer — code reuse:** instead of injecting new code, use **existing, already-executable** code:

- **return-into-libc:** the return address points to a security-critical function of the C library libc (e.g. system("/bin/sh")). DEP does not apply, because libc is legitimately executable. **Limits:** you are restricted to existing functions and can only call them **one after another** (no conditional jumps).
- **Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)** (Shacham, 2007): the generalization. You chain together many tiny code snippets — **gadgets** — each ending in ret. The crafted stack is a list of addresses: each ret jumps to the next gadget. With enough gadgets, ROP becomes **Turing-complete** (arbitrary computation). On x86 the "gadget space" is especially large, because without memory alignment **unintended** instructions also arise in the middle of byte sequences. ROP adds a **new path** through existing nodes to the CFG (no new code). [How to build a ROP chain out of gadgets](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M6lXOVp1brA).

> **Memory aid (the arms race):** **code injection** (your own code) → defense **DEP** (data not executable) → answer **code reuse / ROP** (reuse existing code, DEP-resistant) → defense **ASLR** (hide addresses) + **CFI** (enforce jump targets) → answer **data-oriented** (change no jump at all, only data — bypasses CFI). Remember: **DEP only stops injection, not reuse.**

## Defense: ASLR vs. CFI

Two defense philosophies:

- **Code randomization / ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization):** scrambles the **base addresses** of code and data segments at every start. Then the attacker does not know the addresses of their gadgets/functions. Weaknesses: coarse ASLR is attackable by **brute force** or **memory disclosure** (one leaked address reveals the offset); fine-grained ASLR helps more but is undone by **JIT-ROP**. Advantage: low performance cost.
- **Control-Flow Integrity (CFI)** (Abadi, 2005): enforces that jumps only follow targets that are permitted in the **control-flow graph** (label check). Problem with returns: static labels become coarse-grained → a **shadow stack** (a shadow copy of the return addresses) protects returns in a fine-grained way but costs performance. Advantage: formal security; disadvantage: hard to integrate into complex software.

And the attackers' next move: **data-oriented exploits / non-control-data attacks.** They change **no** control data (no return address, no function pointer) — only a harmless **data variable**, e.g. a field user.id that is set to 0 via an overflow, so that an admin check passes. Because the control flow stays faithful to the CFG, **this bypasses CFI entirely**.

### Step by step: defeating a password check via overflow

This exact data-only attack is the central exercise problem, so let's play it through once. A function pw_check declares two local variables: a buffer char pw_buffer[16] and an integer int auth = 0. It copies the *entire* input with strcpy(pw_buffer, password) — **without a length check** — and sets auth to 1 only if the password is correct; at the end auth is returned.

1. On the stack, pw_buffer and auth sit **directly next to each other** (the order depends on the compiler — which is precisely why the exercise asks what happens with a different arrangement).
2. If you enter an input of **more than 16 characters**, strcpy runs past the end of the buffer and **overwrites auth** with your bytes.
3. As long as those bytes are **not zero**, auth ≠ 0 — the function returns "true," and the program reports "Well done!" **even though your password was wrong**.

This is a pure **data-only attack**: you touched no return address, no code pointer, and no control flow, only overwrote a data variable — which is why neither DEP nor CFI applies here. One defense is the **stack canary** (a random value between the buffer and the critical data, whose alteration exposes the overflow) — but even that can be bypassed if you can read it out or rewrite it deliberately.

## In a nutshell

The short version: here a harmless C mistake turns into a complete takeover of the machine — and a decades-long arms race. The cause is missing bounds checking: a buffer overflow overwrites adjacent stack memory, above all the return address. That is where the attack begins: code injection deposits the attacker's own shellcode and redirects the return address onto it (a new node in the control flow). Against this came DEP (data not executable) — whereupon the attackers responded with code reuse: return-into-libc and above all ROP, which assembles arbitrary computations out of existing gadgets (DEP-resistant). The defense knows two philosophies: ASLR hides the addresses, CFI enforces permitted jump targets (shadow stack for returns). And the attackers' final move is data-oriented exploits, which touch no control data at all — only a harmless variable — and thereby bypass even CFI.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Buffer overflow** | writing past the buffer boundary (no bounds check) |
| **Return address** | the return address saved on the stack; attack target |
| **Shellcode** | injected malicious code (e.g. opening a shell) |
| **EIP / ESP / EBP** | instruction / stack / base pointer |
| **Code injection** | new CFG node (the attacker's own code) |
| **DEP (W^X)** | data not executable / code not writable |
| **return-into-libc** | return into existing libc functions |
| **ROP / gadget** | code reuse from ret-ending snippets, Turing-complete |
| **ASLR** | random base addresses of segments |
| **CFI / shadow stack** | enforce control flow / secure returns |
| **Data-oriented exploit** | change only data → bypasses CFI |

## Typical pitfalls

- **Does the stack grow upward?** No — it grows toward **low** addresses; the overflow runs in the direction of the return address.
- **Does DEP stop all exploits?** No — only **code injection**. **Code reuse** (ret2libc, ROP) bypasses DEP because it uses existing code.
- **Does ROP need injected code?** No — precisely not; ROP uses **only existing** gadgets (which is why it is DEP-resistant).
- **ASLR = secure?** Not necessarily — memory disclosure / JIT-ROP undo it; that is why there is additionally CFI.
- **Does CFI stop everything?** No — **data-oriented exploits** leave the control flow intact and bypass CFI.

## Exam focus

The exercise sheet is very hands-on, so practice the craft. You should be able to **draw a stack-frame layout** (arguments, return address, saved EBP, local variables — including the order of the local variables, because that is decisive in the next point) and explain the role of **ESP/EBP/EIP** as well as call/ret. The central task type is a **concrete vulnerability analysis**: you are given a faulty password check in which **strcpy** copies an overly long string into a buffer that sits directly next to a variable like **auth**. You must name the bug, sketch the stack frame, and **find an input that is not the real password but authenticates anyway** — the overflow overwrites the neighboring variable and sets **auth** to a value ≠ 0 (this is a **data-only attack**; it changes no control data). Also explain what happens if the **compiler changes the variable order**, and how a **stack canary** makes the attack harder (and how it is bypassed). On top of that, conceptually: a **code-injection attack** step by step (overflow → return address → shellcode), **DEP** and why **return-into-libc/ROP** bypass it (code injection = new node, code reuse = new path), **ROP/gadgets** + Turing-completeness, and **ASLR vs. CFI** (including the shadow stack) along with the question of why data-oriented exploits bypass CFI.

## More on this

- **Computerphile — Running a Buffer Overflow Attack** (EN, Mike Pound): shows live how an overflow overwrites the return address and gains root privileges. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1S0aBV-Waeo
- **Motasem Hamdan — Buffer Overflow & ROP Chains (CTF walkthrough)** (EN): how to build a ROP chain out of gadgets. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M6lXOVp1brA
expl:cs-2025-l11:content:
Multics (late 1960s) was the first modern operating system and the birthplace of nearly every security concept we still use today: the reference monitor, protection rings, and multilevel security. We use this historical system as a clean case study for understanding *how* an operating system decides whether a process is allowed to access something. At first glance it is a jungle of terms and rules — but the common thread is a single question: *Who is allowed to do what, and how does the system enforce that mandatorily?*

## Multics & the Reference Monitor

Multics introduced virtual memory, hierarchical file systems — and the core security building blocks. The most important is the **reference monitor**: the combination of hardware and software that **enforces** the security policy. It must satisfy three principles:

1. **Complete Mediation:** **Every** access must go through the reference monitor; bypassing it must be impossible.
2. **Tamperproof (Isolation):** The monitor (and its protection system) must be protected against modification.
3. **Verifiable:** It must be **small enough** to be analyzed and tested — ideally proven.

Multics implements the first two well, but fails at point 3: its **TCB (Trusted Computing Base)** is too large for a formal proof. This is precisely what motivated later **security kernels**, which minimize the TCB.

## Processes, Segments, and the SDW

The basic building blocks are **processes** (which have code and their own virtual address space) and **segments** (subdivisions of the address space: code, data, devices …). Each process has a **descriptor segment** containing **Segment Descriptor Words (SDWs)**; each SDW describes one segment. Loading an SDW is handled by the **supervisor** (the most trusted components, in ring 0/1). An SDW contains:

- the **address** and **length** of the segment,
- the **rights** R/W/E (for the ACL),
- the **ring brackets** (r1, r2, r3) for the ring model,
- the **gate** field: a list of valid entry points into higher-privileged rings.

## Protection Rings

**Protection rings** are a hierarchical layering: **ring 0** = highest privileges (kernel), with ever fewer the further out you go. Multics offers 64 rings (8 in hardware, 56 in software); modern CPUs (Intel) have 4, but usually only **ring 0 (kernel)** and **ring 3 (user)** are used. Higher-privileged rings may access the memory of lower rings. A **code segment** is executable; a **gate segment** is a special code segment that, via the gate field, offers defined **entry points** into a more privileged ring — there, the arguments from the lower-privileged caller are **validated** (protection against malicious input). Typical use case: a **system call**.

![Protection rings of the x86 architecture: ring 0 (kernel) inside, ring 3 (applications) outside](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/25/CPU_ring_scheme.svg "Protection rings: ring 0 (kernel) right at the center = most privileged, with power decreasing outward down to ring 3 (applications). Modern operating systems use only ring 0 and ring 3.")

> **Mnemonic (protection rings):** **smaller = more powerful.** Ring **0 = core (innermost, everything allowed)**, with power decreasing outward; ring 3 = ordinary programs. You can only enter a *more powerful* (smaller) ring through a **gate** (= the system call), which checks your arguments.

## The Three Access Models: ACL, Rings & Brackets, Multilevel Security

For every access, **all three** models must agree:

- **Access Control List (ACL)** — *DAC* (Discretionary Access Control, at the owner's discretion): a list of users and rights per segment/directory. User ID = **Person.Project.Tag** (e.g. Jim.ProjectX.*; * is a wildcard). Rights for segments: r/w/e/null; for directories: status/modify/append.
- **Rings & Brackets** — also *DAC*: govern, via the current ring number r and the segment's brackets, whether reading/writing/executing is allowed.
- **Multilevel Security** — *MAC* (Mandatory Access Control, system-wide and immutable): the classification levels (see below).

### Working through Rings & Brackets

Two bracket pairs control access (current ring number = r; smaller = more privileged):

- **Access Bracket (r1, r2)** with r1 ≤ r2 — governs **reading/writing**:
  - r ≤ r1: **read and write**
  - r1 < r ≤ r2: **read only**
  - r2 < r: **no access**
- **Call Bracket (r2, r3)** — governs **executing**: r3 is the highest ring allowed to execute the code. If **r2 < r3**, it is a **gate segment**; if **r2 = r3**, a normal code segment.

The four cases of the execution check (a process in ring r wants to execute a code segment with (r1,r2),(r2,r3)):

| Case | Result |
|---|---|
| **r3 < r** | execution **forbidden** (outside the permitted range) |
| **r2 < r ≤ r3** | allowed **only through a gate**; ring switch to the more privileged **r' = r2** (e.g. system call) |
| **r1 ≤ r ≤ r2** | allowed, **no** ring switch |
| **r < r1** | allowed; ring switch to the less privileged **r' = r1** |

Example (r = 4): Access Bracket (5, 6) → r < r1, so read+write; (3, 4) → r1 < r ≤ r2, so read only; (3, 3) → r2 < r, so no access.

> **Mnemonic (brackets):** **Access Bracket = data (read/write)**, **Call Bracket = code (execute)**. For reading/writing: the further *out* (the larger r) you are, the less you may do — first RW, then only R, then nothing. **You recognize a gate segment by the Call Bracket r2 < r3.** These "four cases" are the classic computational exercise.

### Step by step: a complete access decision

The eleven cases on the exercise sheet are built exactly this way, so here is one played through in full. Question: May **process P4 (current ring number r = 2)** execute the **code segment D** with Access Bracket **(1, 3)** and Call Bracket **(3, 5)** — and does P4 belong to a user who, per the **ACL**, has execute rights (e)?

1. **First the ACL.** Is there an entry in the segment's access list with **e** that matches P4's user ID (Person.Project.Tag, possibly with a wildcard)? If not → immediately forbidden, regardless of what the brackets say. Let's assume the ACL permits it.
2. **Gate segment?** Call Bracket (3, 5): r2 = 3 < r3 = 5 → yes, it is a gate segment (so a ring switch is in principle possible).
3. **Which of the four cases?** Currently r = 2. Check in order: r3 < r? 5 < 2 → no. r2 < r ≤ r3? 3 < 2 → no. r1 ≤ r ≤ r2? **1 ≤ 2 ≤ 3 → yes.** → execution **allowed, without a ring switch** (P4 continues running in ring 2).

Two variants to check yourself: if P4 were in **ring 0** (r < r1 = 1), it would also be allowed to execute, but with a **ring switch to r' = r1 = 1** (downgrade). If P4 were in **ring 4** (r2 = 3 < 4 ≤ r3 = 5), it would only be possible **through the gate**, with a **ring switch to r' = r2 = 3** (upgrade — the typical system call). If in doing so the caller passes a **pointer to memory in another ring**, the gate must additionally validate it.

## Multilevel Security & Bell-LaPadula

Multics was a pioneer of **multilevel security (MLS)**: every segment and every process is assigned a **classification level** (top-secret, secret, confidential, unclassified). The policy enforced is the **Bell-LaPadula model**, which prevents **information leakage**. The two core rules:

- **Read (no read up):** A process may only read from segments of **equal or lower** level.
- **Write (no write down):** A process may only write to segments of **equal or higher** level.

> **Mnemonic (Bell-LaPadula):** **"no read up, no write down"** — those who know secrets must not leak them downward. Picture: a general may **read orders downward** (read down) and **report upward** (write up), but **must not** write secret info to subordinates. Goal: no **information leakage**. Details on the [Wikipedia page on Bell-LaPadula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell-LaPadula_model).

This prevents secret information from "trickling down" into a lower level. (Multics' legacy is enormous: after Bell left, Thompson and Ritchie built **UNIX**; commands like ls, pwd, mail come from Multics.)

## In a Nutshell

The short version: Multics shows, through a historical example, *how* an operating system mandatorily enforces access. At its heart is the reference monitor, which checks every access and must satisfy three principles: Complete Mediation (cannot be bypassed), Tamperproof (tamper-resistant), and Verifiable (small enough to be proven — which Multics' large TCB fails at). A process consists of segments, which are described via Segment Descriptor Words (address, length, rights, ring brackets, gate). Protection rings are hierarchical (ring 0 = kernel, most privileged); you can only enter a more powerful ring through a gate segment, which checks the arguments. For every access, three models must agree simultaneously: ACL and Rings & Brackets (both DAC) as well as multilevel security (MAC). The latter implements Bell-LaPadula — "no read up, no write down" — and thus prevents the leakage of secret information.

## Terms & Notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Reference Monitor** | checks every access; Complete Mediation, Tamperproof, Verifiable |
| **TCB** | Trusted Computing Base (must be small for provability) |
| **Segment / SDW** | address-space part / Segment Descriptor Word (address, length, rights, brackets, gate) |
| **Supervisor** | most trusted components (ring 0/1) |
| **Protection ring** | privilege layer; ring 0 = highest |
| **Gate segment** | entry point into a higher ring (r2 < r3), validates arguments |
| **ACL** | users+rights per segment; user ID Person.Project.Tag (DAC) |
| **Access Bracket (r1,r2)** | r≤r1 RW, r1<r≤r2 R, r2<r no access |
| **Call Bracket (r2,r3)** | execution; r2<r3 ⇒ gate segment |
| **DAC / MAC** | at discretion / system-wide mandatory |
| **Bell-LaPadula** | MLS model: no read up, no write down |

## Common Pitfalls

- **Ring 0 = least privileged?** No — **ring 0 is the most privileged** (kernel); privilege decreases outward.
- **How do you recognize a gate segment?** By the Call Bracket: **r2 < r3** ⇒ gate segment; r2 = r3 ⇒ normal segment.
- **Bell-LaPadula: you may write downward?** No — **no write down** (write only equal/higher), **no read up** (read only equal/lower).
- **Does the ACL alone decide?** No — **all three** models (ACL, Rings & Brackets, MLS) must agree.
- **Is Multics formally verified?** No — the **TCB is too large**; that's why it satisfies Verifiable only inadequately.

## Exam Focus

By far the most important task is the **access decision by hand** — the exercise sheet works through eleven cases, and that's how it appears on the exam. You are given processes with a current ring number r, user IDs (Person.Project.Tag), and segments with an **ACL** *and* **ring brackets**. For "may process P access segment S for reading/writing/executing?" you check **both** models: first whether the ACL permits the user for the operation (with wildcards like *.ProjectA.a), then the brackets. For the **Access Bracket (r1, r2)**: r ≤ r1 → read+write, r1 < r ≤ r2 → read only, r2 < r → no access. For the **Call Bracket (r2, r3)** for execution, the four cases (r3 < r forbidden; r2 < r ≤ r3 only through a gate, switch to r' = r2; r1 ≤ r ≤ r2 allowed without a switch; r < r1 allowed, switch to r' = r1). When an access is allowed, **also state whether/to which ring the process switches**. A special case: if a **pointer to memory in another ring** is passed during a gate call, the gate must check it as well (argument validation). On top of that, the theory: why rings are needed *in addition to* ACLs, what **gate segments** accomplish and why r2 < r3 must hold for them; the **three reference monitor principles**; **DAC vs. MAC**; and **Bell-LaPadula** ("no read up, no write down"). Related on the same sheet: **Linux file permissions** (rwx for user/group/other, chmod 444/777/641, the **SUID/SGID** flag, what execute/write means on a *directory*).

## Further Reading

- **Wikipedia — Bell-LaPadula model** (EN): the read/write rules (no read up, no write down) and the MAC background. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell-LaPadula_model
- **Saltzer & Schroeder — A Hardware Architecture for Implementing Protection Rings** (EN): the original source on protection rings. https://www.multicians.org/protection.html
- **multicians.org** (EN): the history and concepts of Multics firsthand. https://www.multicians.org/
expl:cs-2025-l12:content:
How do you understand a program when all you have is the finished binary — say, a suspicious .exe? That's reverse engineering, and its most important application is malware analysis. The lecture first explains *why* this is hard (compilation throws away almost all meaning), then *what* malware does (infection, persistence, types, ransomware), and finally the cat-and-mouse game: how malware authors make analysis harder — and how analysts fight back.

## Reverse engineering: from the binary back to understanding

When building an application, C/C++ code passes through a chain: **preprocessor → compiler → assembler → linker**. Out comes machine code in which **all human-readable information is lost** — variable types and names, function names, and comments only exist (if at all) as debug info. That's exactly what makes reverse engineering hard: you have to reconstruct intent from raw assembly. Applications: checking functionality, finding bugs/vulnerabilities, **patching programs without source code**, and above all **detecting malicious software**.

![From source code through the compiler to the executable program](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6b/Compiler.svg "Compilation translates readable source code into machine code — discarding names, types, and comments along the way. Reverse engineering tries to walk that path backwards.")

> **Memory aid (static vs. dynamic):** **static = look at it** (don't run it → strings, disassembly, CFG). **dynamic = run it** (debugger, sandbox/VM, observe behaviour). Rule of thumb: first strings/static for a quick overview, then dynamic for the real behaviour. [Low Level shows exactly these stages](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8vk5z9VAaBQ).

## What is malware?

**Malware**, according to NIST, is a program that is (usually covertly) inserted to compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim's data/systems. Mostly for Windows. A short timeline: Morris worm (1988), ILOVEYOU (2000), Zeus (2009), Cryptolocker (2013), Mirai (2016). The analysis has clear **goals**: the **infection path** (downloads, email attachments, exploits in browser/PDF/Word/OS), **persistence**, **function/type**, **removal**, and bypassing anti-analysis.

**Persistence** (running code even after a reboot) is achieved by Windows malware mainly through: the **autostart folder**, **registry run keys** (e.g. HKLMSoftwareMicrosoftWindowsCurrentVersionRun), and **services** (background processes that start automatically). For **removal** you begin right here — at the persistence mechanism — and work your way out to all components.

Malware **types**: trojan, worm, virus, cryptominer, backdoor, bot, adware, **ransomware**.

## Ransomware in detail

**Ransomware** encrypts the victim's files (hard drive, network drives, backups) and demands a **ransom** (usually in cryptocurrency) for decryption. Its peculiarity: it does **not** have to be stealthy and needs **no persistence** — the damage is done after a single run. Typical implementation traces (great for analysis!): file search (FindFirstFile/FindNextFile), filtering for specific extensions, encryption (AES/CryptEncrypt), **deleting backups** (vssadmin delete shadows), writing a **ransom note**. Common **author mistakes** that make cracking possible: homemade/weak crypto, **non-random** or static keys, keys derived from known info (username, timestamp, MAC address), keys that are too short. [Computerphile dissects WannaCry](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=88jkB1V6N9w).

> **Memory aid (ransomware):** the only malware that is **loud rather than quiet** — no stealth, no persistence needed, because after *one* run the files are encrypted. It is almost always cracked via **bad keys** (static, predictable, too short), not via the encryption algorithm.

## Making static analysis harder

**Static analysis** examines the program without running it — the most productive things are **strings** (error messages, paths, domains reveal a lot) and the **disassembly** (with control-flow graph). Malware authors counter with **obfuscation**:

- **Garbage code:** useless instructions (NOP equivalents, self-cancelling operations) as a "needle in a haystack".
- **String obfuscation:** strings are stored unreadable and only decrypted **at runtime** — tools like strings.exe come up empty. (The obfuscation doesn't have to be "secure", just unreadable.)
- **Code obfuscation:** parts of the code exist in obfuscated form; a **stub** decrypts them at runtime in memory and jumps into them.
- **PE obfuscation:** the **entire** binary is obfuscated (code, data, strings, imports, header) — statically, nothing can be meaningfully analysed anymore; a stub restores the original at runtime.

### Step by step: decrypting an obfuscated C&C domain name

This is exactly the task the exercise sheet poses: a piece of malware wants to resolve a domain in order to reach its **command-and-control server**; as an analyst you want to find out the name so you can block it. But in the binary you don't find "evil-server.com", you find the obfuscated string **fwjm.tfswfs/dpn**. That looks like a shift — and indeed every character is shifted by **+1** in the character set. So you implement the deobfuscation algorithm by shifting every character **back by 1**: f→e, w→v, j→i, m→l, .→-, t→s, f→e, s→r, w→v, f→e, s→r, /→., d→c, p→o, n→m. Out comes **evil-server.com**. Remember the procedure: pull the obfuscated string out of the binary → recognise the (often simple) transformation → reverse it. The obfuscation precisely does not have to be *secure*, just unreadable enough that the tool **strings** alone reveals nothing.

## Making dynamic analysis harder

**Dynamic analysis** executes (parts of) the code and observes the behaviour (functions called, file/registry accesses, internet connections) — with a **debugger**, monitors, and **sandboxes/VMs**. A **debugger** allows controlled execution, reading memory/registers, and manipulating data. Malware counters by **detecting** the analysis environment:

- **Detecting a debugger:** via the Windows API IsDebuggerPresent or directly the PEB flag (mov eax, fs:[0x30] …). Bypass: set the flag to 0.
- **Detecting breakpoints:** debuggers replace instruction bytes with **0xCC** (INT 3) — the program searches its own memory for 0xCC or checks a **checksum** over the code; additionally there are hardware breakpoints (debug registers DR0–DR3).
- **Detecting a sandbox/VM:** via **artefacts** (traces that real hardware doesn't have) or **timing** (code runs measurably differently in the VM).

Important lesson: these checks are often implemented **sloppily** — every check, on detection, simply calls exit(). Then you don't even have to understand the anti-analysis, you just **patch** all the checks away.

> **Memory aid (anti-analysis):** **static** is countered with **obfuscation** (garbage, string, code, PE obfuscation), **dynamic** with **detection** (IsDebuggerPresent, 0xCC bytes = INT 3, VM/timing). But: a check that only calls **exit()** is **patchable away** — you don't need to understand it.

## In a nutshell

The short version: reverse engineering is the inverse of compilation — and it's hard because the binary no longer contains names, types, or comments. You work with two kinds of analysis: static (look without executing — strings, disassembly, CFG) and dynamic (run and observe — debugger, sandbox/VM). The most important application is malware analysis, with the goals infection path, persistence, function/type, and removal. Persistence (surviving a reboot) is achieved by Windows malware via autostart, registry run keys, or services; ransomware is the loud special case (no stealth/persistence needed, cracked via bad keys). Malware authors make analysis harder with obfuscation (garbage code, string/code/PE obfuscation) against static analysis and with debugger/VM detection against dynamic analysis — both often so sloppy that you simply patch the checks away.

## Terms & notation

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **reverse engineering** | inverse of compilation; reconstructing intent from a binary |
| **static analysis** | without execution: strings, disassembly, CFG |
| **dynamic analysis** | with execution: debugger, sandbox/VM |
| **malware** | malicious software (compromises C/I/A) |
| **persistence** | surviving a reboot (autostart, run keys, services) |
| **ransomware** | encrypts files, demands a ransom |
| **obfuscation** | making code/strings/PE unreadable (stub at runtime) |
| **0xCC / INT 3** | software breakpoint byte |
| **IsDebuggerPresent** | API for debugger detection |
| **sandbox/VM detection** | artefact- or timing-based |

## Common traps

- **Does the binary still contain source code?** No — names/comments are gone; at most debug info remains.
- **Does ransomware need persistence/stealth?** No — the damage is done after one run; it's allowed to be loud.
- **Does string obfuscation have to be cryptographically secure?** No — it only has to be **unreadable** (deobfuscation at runtime).
- **Does PE obfuscation only make strings harder?** No — it obfuscates **the entire** binary (code, data, imports, header).
- **Does anti-analysis make malware uncrackable?** No — poorly made checks can simply be **patched** (remove exit()).

## Exam focus

The exercise sheet is practical and revolves around two things you have to be able to explain. First, **static analysis with the tool **strings****: from the extracted character strings of an unknown file you should recognise **what program it is and what it does** (error messages, paths, domains, imported functions reveal a lot) — and explain **why there is so much "garbage" among the strings** (machine-code bytes that happen to look like printable characters). Second, **string obfuscation using the malware example**: a piece of malware resolves a **DNS domain** to make contact with a **command-and-control server**; the domain name is stored obfuscated. You should extract the obfuscated value and **trace/reimplement the deobfuscation algorithm** to obtain the plaintext domain name (which you would then block). On top of that, more conceptually: the **compilation chain** and why information is lost in it, **static vs. dynamic** with their tools, the malware analysis goals and the three **persistence** mechanisms, **ransomware** (process + weak keys), and the **anti-analysis** tricks (types of obfuscation; detection via IsDebuggerPresent, 0xCC bytes, VM/timing) — along with the insight that sloppy checks are simply patchable away.

## More on this

- **Computerphile — Wana Decrypt0r (WannaCry Ransomware)** (EN, Mike Pound): dissects a real piece of ransomware and explains how it encrypts files. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=88jkB1V6N9w
- **Low Level — Every Level of Reverse Engineering Explained** (EN): from strings through disassembly to behavioural analysis — exactly the stages of the lecture. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8vk5z9VAaBQ
- **Jane Street — Reverse Engineering Ransomware (M. Sikorski)** (EN): the author of "Practical Malware Analysis" walks through the analysis process on real samples. https://www.janestreet.com/tech-talks/dude-where-are-my-files/
expl:cs-2025-l13:content:
The last session is a review — and exactly the right tool for exam preparation. Instead of new material, this chapter bundles up the **central thread of the entire course**: the three pillars, how the individual lectures build on one another, and a compact checklist of what you really need to be able to do. Use it as a map and jump from here back into the individual chapter explanations.

The whole course rests on three pillars, framed by the foundational concepts from Lecture 1. **Cryptography** (L2–L8) secures the data, **network and web security** (L9) secures the communication, and **software and system security** (L10–L12) secures the programs and systems. Within crypto there runs a clear thread — from symmetric (DES, AES) through asymmetric (RSA and the key exchange problem) to integrity (hashes, signatures, MAC) and on to the applications (Bitcoin, protocols, TLS). And running through all of it are the same principles from the first chapter: the CIA triad, the weakest link, defense in depth, Kerckhoffs, and the adversarial setting. Let's go through the three pillars one by one.

![The CIA triad as the central thread of the entire course](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c5/CIAJMK1209-en.svg "The same protection goals hover over all three pillars: confidentiality, integrity, availability. Almost every attack in the course can be assigned to one of them.")

## Pillar 1: Cryptography (L2–L8)

The arc is clean: it begins with **classical ciphers** and the lesson that a large key space is not enough (frequency analysis, L2). Then **symmetric block ciphers**: **DES** with the Feistel structure (L3) and **AES** with its four layers (L4) — both following the principle of confusion + diffusion over many rounds. Because symmetric methods have the **key exchange problem**, along comes **asymmetric** cryptography: **RSA** based on factorization (L5). For integrity and authenticity follow **hash functions, digital signatures, and MAC/HMAC** (L6). The conclusion is formed by two major applications: **Bitcoin** (hashes + signatures + proof-of-work, L7) and **key agreement/protocols** — Diffie-Hellman, Needham-Schroeder, Kerberos (L8).

## Pillar 2: Network & Web Security (L9)

Here the course leaves the mathematics behind. **TLS** brings the crypto together on the web (hybrid). Two types of attacker: **denial-of-service** attacks **availability** (ICMP flooding, spoofing, reflection/amplification, DDoS via botnet like Mirai), and **input validation** addresses malicious inputs (**XSS**, **SQL injection**) — fended off through sanitization, validation, **whitelisting**, and CSP.

## Pillar 3: Software & System Security (L10–L12)

First the **software exploits** (L10): a buffer overflow overwrites the return address; out of this comes code injection, and in the arms race with DEP arise **code reuse/ROP** as well as the defenses **ASLR** and **CFI**. Then **operating system security** using the example of **Multics** (L11): **reference monitor**, **protection rings**, ACL, and **Bell-LaPadula** show how an OS strictly enforces access. Finally **reverse engineering & malware** (L12): how to analyze binaries without source code (static/dynamic) and how malware (persistence, ransomware) evades analysis.

> **Memory aid (the whole course in one sentence):** **Math → Wire → Machine.** Pillar 1 secures the *data* (crypto), pillar 2 the *communication* (network/web), pillar 3 the *programs & systems* (exploits, OS, malware) — and over it all hover the principles from L1 (CIA, weakest link, adversarial setting). For reviewing individual topics, the [Computerphile channel](https://www.youtube.com/computerphile) is worth its weight in gold.

## Terms & Notation

| Pillar | Must-know terms |
|---|---|
| **Foundations** | CIA triad, weakest link, defense in depth, threat model, Kerckhoffs |
| **Symmetric** | confusion/diffusion, Feistel (DES), layers (AES), ECB/CBC, XOR, OTP |
| **Asymmetric** | one-way function, RSA (n,e,d,φ(n)), factorization, Diffie-Hellman (discrete log) |
| **Integrity** | hash (preimage/collision resistance), birthday paradox 2^(n/2), signature, MAC/HMAC, certificate/PKI |
| **Applications** | Bitcoin (hash pointer, Merkle, PoW, UTXO), TLS, KDC, replay, Kerberos |
| **Network/Web** | DoS/DDoS, reflection/amplification, XSS, SQL injection, whitelisting, CSP |
| **Software/System** | buffer overflow, DEP, ROP, ASLR, CFI; reference monitor, rings/brackets, Bell-LaPadula; malware, persistence, ransomware |

## Common Pitfalls

- **Learning topics in isolation.** The strength of the exam lies in the **cross-connections** (e.g. „why does Bitcoin need hashes *and* signatures?", „why does asymmetric solve the problem of symmetric?").
- **Confusing concepts vs. details.** The professor emphasizes: **understand concepts**, don't just memorize implementation details (e.g. read PHP code, don't write it).
- **Forgetting the CIA assignment.** Many attacks can be assigned to a protection goal (DoS → availability, sniffing → confidentiality, manipulation → integrity).
- **Underestimating the computational problems.** RSA, Diffie-Hellman, the DES round, and rings & brackets are typical **computational problems** — practice them, don't just read about them.

## Exam Focus

- **Be able to compute:** RSA (key generation + encryption/decryption + EEA + square-and-multiply), Diffie-Hellman (k_AB = α^(a·b) mod p), one DES Feistel round, **rings & brackets** (the four cases).
- **Be able to explain:** confusion/diffusion, hash security properties + birthday paradox, signature vs. MAC, MITM + certificates, double-spend + longest chain, DoS variants, XSS vs. SQLi, code injection vs. code reuse, DEP/ASLR/CFI, reference monitor principles, Bell-LaPadula.
- **Be able to classify:** assign every mechanism to the appropriate **CIA** property and the correct **pillar**.
- Go through the individual chapter explanations (L1–L12) and test yourself against each one's **Exam Focus** — this summary is the map for it.

## Further Reading

- **Computerphile (YouTube channel)** (EN): short, high-quality videos on almost all course topics — ideal for review. https://www.youtube.com/computerphile
- **Patrick Winston (MIT) — „How To Speak"** (EN): how to present content clearly — useful for oral exams and presentations. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Unzc731iCUY
- **David JP Phillips — „How to avoid death by PowerPoint"** (EN): how to present content convincingly. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Iwpi1Lm6dFo
expl:cs-2025-u-prep:content:
Before anything gets encrypted in this course, you need two computational tools that will reappear in *every* single exercise afterwards: computing with remainders (**modulo**) and computing with zeros and ones (**binary**). This sheet has no official sample solution — it is pure preparation. That is exactly why it pays to see every step fully worked out here just once, because Caesar, Vigenère, RSA, DES, and even Bitcoin mining ultimately come down to precisely these two operations.

## Modulo is nothing but „doing math on a clock"

Picture a clock. It's 12 o'clock, and you wait 100 hours. You don't end up at „112 o'clock" — the hands turn in a circle and land somewhere between 0 and 23. That is exactly what modulo is: you don't care about the whole number, only about the **remainder** that is left over when you keep subtracting one full lap (one full „modulus").

The one formula on which everything rests is **division with remainder**. If you divide a number x by a divisor d, then x can always be uniquely decomposed like this:

> **Key rule:** x = q · d + r, where q is the integer quotient (the full laps) and r is the remainder, with 0 ≤ r < d. The remainder r is exactly what „x mod d" yields.

The example from the sheet: 47 mod 7. How many times does 7 fit fully into 47? Six times, since 6 · 7 = 42. What's left over? 47 − 42 = 5. So 47 = 6 · 7 + 5, and therefore **47 mod 7 = 5**. The trick is always the same: find the largest multiple of the modulus that still fits, and read off the remainder.

### Step by step — Exercise 1

**(a) It's April. Which month is it in 50 months?** Months repeat every 12. April is month number 4. So we don't want to read 4 + 50 = 54 as „month 54", but rather fold it back into the range 1–12. Compute 54 mod 12: how many times does 12 fit into 54? Four times, since 4 · 12 = 48. Remainder: 54 − 48 = 6. Month 6 is **June**. (Quick way: 50 = 4·12 + 2, so four full years plus two months; April + 2 months = June.)

**(b) It's 13:00. What time is it in 100 hours?** Hours repeat every 24. First fold the 100 hours back: 100 mod 24. We have 4 · 24 = 96, remainder 100 − 96 = 4. So in 100 hours there are effectively 4 hours of „real" movement on the clock. 13 + 4 = 17, and 17 mod 24 = 17. Answer: **17:00**.

Note the subtle but important point: in (b) you still have to add the starting value (13) *before* you're done. For pure „in X days/months from a starting point" problems you can also add everything first and then take the modulus once — both give the same result, because modulo is compatible with addition. This is no coincidence, but a rule that saves our lives later with RSA: (a + b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n.

## Binary — the language in which every computer really thinks

Our decimal system has ten digits (0–9), and each position is a power of ten: 372 means 3·100 + 7·10 + 2·1, that is 3·10² + 7·10¹ + 2·10⁰. A computer knows only two states, power on / power off, so only two digits: 0 and 1. The same idea, but with **base 2**: each position is a power of two.

Memorize the small powers of two by heart, they are your ruler: 2⁰=1, 2¹=2, 2²=4, 2³=8, 2⁴=16, 2⁵=32, 2⁶=64, 2⁷=128, 2⁸=256, 2⁹=512, 2¹⁰=1024.

### Step by step — binary to decimal (Exercise 2a)

You read the binary number from right to left and add the corresponding power of two everywhere there is a 1. Lay the bits out over their place values:

| Binary | Calculation (only the 1-positions) | Decimal |
|---|---|---|
| 1111 | 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 | **15** |
| 10001 | 16 + 1 | **17** |
| 101010 | 32 + 8 + 2 | **42** |
| 101 | 4 + 1 | **5** |

For 101010, for example, the ones are at positions 2⁵, 2³ and 2¹ (counted from the right starting at 0), so 32 + 8 + 2 = 42.

### Step by step — decimal to binary (Exercise 2b)

Here you use the **division with remainder** from above — but repeatedly by 2. You keep dividing the number by 2, write down the remainder each time (0 or 1), and stop when you reach 0. The remainders read **from bottom to top** are the binary number. Example with 27:

| Step | Calculation | Remainder |
|---|---|---|
| 27 / 2 | = 13 | **1** |
| 13 / 2 | = 6 | **1** |
| 6 / 2 | = 3 | **0** |
| 3 / 2 | = 1 | **1** |
| 1 / 2 | = 0 | **1** |

From bottom to top: 11011. Check (converting back): 16 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 27. ✓

The remaining three by the same method (or, faster, by collecting the matching powers of two):

- **127** = 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = **1111111** (seven ones — typical for „one less than a power of two").
- **128** = 2⁷ = **10000000** (one 1, then seven zeros — exactly one power of two). Nice to see: 127 and 128 lie right next to each other and look completely different in binary.
- **2025**: subtract the largest powers of two. 2025 − 1024 = 1001; − 512 = 489; − 256 = 233; − 128 = 105; − 64 = 41; − 32 = 9; − 8 = 1; − 1 = 0. The powers used were 2¹⁰, 2⁹, 2⁸, 2⁷, 2⁶, 2⁵, 2³, 2⁰. This gives **11111101001**. Check: 1024+512+256+128+64+32+8+1 = 2025. ✓

> **Memory aid:** „one less than a power of two" is always an unbroken chain of ones (127 = 1111111), „a power of two itself" is a single 1 with nothing but zeros behind it (128 = 10000000).

### Step by step — binary addition with carry (Exercise 2c)

Adding works exactly like in the decimal system, from right to left — except that the carry already arises at 1 + 1, since 1 + 1 = 10 in binary (i.e. „write down 0, carry the 1"). Let's compute 10101 + 11110:

| Position (from the right) | top | bottom | carry in | sum | written down | carry out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **1** | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **1** | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 10 | **0** | 1 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 10 | **0** | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 11 | **1** | 1 |
| 6 | — | — | 1 | 1 | **1** | 0 |

Read from top to bottom (highest position first): **110011**. Check in the decimal system: 10101 = 21, 11110 = 30, and 110011 = 32 + 16 + 2 + 1 = 51. Indeed 21 + 30 = 51. ✓ „What do you have to watch out for?" — exactly the **carry**: as soon as two ones (or three) come together, a 1 moves into the next position.

## What you must reliably be able to do after this sheet

These two operations are no side matter — they are the backbone of half the semester. Modulo 26 is Caesar and Vigenère; modulo n is RSA; thinking in binary is what you need for DES, for XOR stream ciphers, and for reading memory addresses in the exploit chapters. If you read „x mod d" as „the remainder after full laps" and can convert binary numbers fluently in both directions, then you have already cleared the computational hurdle where most people lose time in the exam.
expl:cs-2025-u01:content:
This sheet is your first real encounter with encryption by hand. It walks you through exactly three historical methods, and they are deliberately chosen in this order: Caesar shows the basic principle *and* immediately its weakness, transposition turns the principle on its head, and Vigenère repairs the Caesar weakness. Anyone who can compute these three by hand will afterwards understand every substitution and permutation idea in the course. We solve each task the way the professor works it out on the board: first the rule, then every single letter.

One thing up front that *all* three methods share: each letter is assigned a number, A = 0, B = 1, …, Z = 25. All the arithmetic then happens "modulo 26", because the alphabet has 26 letters and we want to land back at A again after Z — the same clock principle as on the fundamentals sheet.

## Task 1 — Caesar (shift cipher) and why it gets exposed

Caesar is a **substitution cipher**: each letter is *replaced* by another by shifting it a fixed number k forward in the alphabet. The rule, stated formally on the sheet and which you must know by heart:

> **Mnemonic:** Encrypting e_k(x) = (x + k) mod 26. Decrypting d_k(y) = (y − k) mod 26. That's all Caesar is.

In the task the key is k = 5. Let's take the first word of the plaintext, **Sehr**, and encrypt it letter by letter — this is exactly the step that the model solution gives as a finished block but does not work out:

### Step by step — Caesar with k = 5

| Plaintext | Number x | x + 5 | mod 26 | Ciphertext |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S | 18 | 23 | 23 | **X** |
| e | 4 | 9 | 9 | **j** |
| h | 7 | 12 | 12 | **m** |
| r | 17 | 22 | 22 | **w** |

So **Sehr** becomes the **Xjmw** of the model solution. You repeat this for every letter; it only gets interesting in one place: when x + 5 runs past 25. Example V: V = 21, 21 + 5 = 26, and 26 mod 26 = 0 = **A**. This is exactly where you see why "mod 26" has to be there — without the remainder there would be no letter anymore.

**Why Caesar is so easy to break (tasks 3–5, frequency analysis).** Caesar shifts *all* letters by the same amount. That means: the most frequent character in the plaintext also remains the most frequent in the ciphertext — it just has a different name. In German, E is by far the most frequent (around 17 %). If you do a **frequency analysis** (you count how often each character occurs and draw a bar chart), then the chart of the ciphertext is exactly the same as that of the plaintext, only shifted sideways — by exactly k positions. So an attacker simply looks for the highest bar, assumes "that's the E", measures the shift, and has k. That's also why in task 5 you observe: the *longer* the text, the more its distribution resembles the official German frequency table — short texts fluctuate, long texts carry more statistics within them and reveal the key all the more reliably.

> **Memory aid:** A monoalphabetic cipher *shifts* the frequency histogram, it does not *destroy* it. That is precisely its death sentence.

## Task 2 — Columnar transposition

Now the principle is reversed. A **transposition cipher** does not replace letters, it *rearranges their order*. The letters stay the same — a frequency analysis therefore looks completely normal — but they sit in different places. In **columnar transposition** you write the text row by row into a grid with as many columns as the key has letters, and then read the columns out again in the **alphabetical order of the key letters**.

The example from the sheet shows the encryption: plaintext **Beispiele**, key **HAL**. In three columns (B/E/I, S/P/I, E/L/E). If you sort H, A, L alphabetically into A, H, L, then the A column (that was column 2) comes first, then the H column (column 1), then the L column (column 3) — result EBIPSILEE.

**Task 2.1 asks: how do you decrypt that again?** The answer, and this is the whole trick: you **reverse the permutation**. In encryption, column 1 moved to position 2, column 3 to position 3, and so on. In decryption you undo exactly this mapping — what was "column 1 → position 2" in encryption now becomes "position 2 → column 1".

### Step by step — decrypting YRCOTPCSILOO with key SEC

**Step 1 — determine the dimensions.** The ciphertext has 12 characters, the key **SEC** has 3 letters → 3 columns, so 12 / 3 = 4 rows. The ciphertext fills the columns *in alphabetical key order* from top to bottom. Ordered alphabetically, SEC → **C, E, S**.

**Step 2 — enter the ciphertext block by block into the sorted columns.** We cut YRCOTPCSILOO into three blocks of 4 characters each (YRCO, TPCS, ILOO) and place them under C, E, S:

| C column | E column | S column |
|---|---|---|
| Y | T | I |
| R | P | L |
| C | C | O |
| O | S | O |

**Step 3 — sort the columns back into the original key order.** The key was originally **S-E-C**. So the S column (I, L, O, O) belongs in the real column 1, the E column (T, P, C, S) in column 2, and the C column (Y, R, C, O) in column 3. Assembled row by row:

| Column 1 (S) | Column 2 (E) | Column 3 (C) |
|---|---|---|
| C | R | Y |
| P | T | O |
| I | S | C |
| O | O | L |

**Step 4 — read row by row.** C-R-Y, P-T-O, I-S-C, O-O-L gives **CRYPTOISCOOL** — "crypto is cool". This is exactly the result of the model solution. The observation the professor notes: column 1 became column 3 during encryption and vice versa — we just ran this swap backwards.

> **Common pitfall:** In transposition you **permute, you don't shift**. Anyone who tries (as with Caesar) to shift letters by k here has confused the methods. The letters stay unchanged — only their places swap.

## Task 3 — Vigenère (polyalphabetic substitution)

Vigenère repairs Caesar's weakness with a simple idea: instead of *one* shift for the whole text, you use *many* — one per key letter, and the key repeats cyclically. So it's "Caesar, but the key changes with every letter". The key **SICHER** yields the shifts S=18, I=8, C=2, H=7, E=4, R=17; after that it starts over from the beginning.

The rule per position is again the Caesar formula, except that k now comes from the key letter at *this* position: e(x) = (x + k_i) mod 26. The Vigenère square on the sheet is just a lookup table for exactly this addition (row = plaintext letter, column = key letter).

### Step by step — encrypting "Cybersicherheitsvorlesung" with SICHER

Write the key character by character under the plaintext (repeated cyclically) and add modulo 26. Here the first eleven positions worked out in full — the rest runs identically:

| # | Plaintext | x | Key | k | x + k | mod 26 | Ciphertext |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C | 2 | S | 18 | 20 | 20 | **U** |
| 2 | y | 24 | I | 8 | 32 | 6 | **G** |
| 3 | b | 1 | C | 2 | 3 | 3 | **D** |
| 4 | e | 4 | H | 7 | 11 | 11 | **L** |
| 5 | r | 17 | E | 4 | 21 | 21 | **V** |
| 6 | s | 18 | R | 17 | 35 | 9 | **J** |
| 7 | i | 8 | S | 18 | 26 | 0 | **A** |
| 8 | c | 2 | I | 8 | 10 | 10 | **K** |
| 9 | h | 7 | C | 2 | 9 | 9 | **J** |
| 10 | e | 4 | H | 7 | 11 | 11 | **L** |
| 11 | r | 17 | E | 4 | 21 | 21 | **V** |

Note positions 6 and 7: 18 + 17 = 35, and 35 mod 26 = 9 → J; 8 + 18 = 26, and 26 mod 26 = 0 → A. Whenever the sum reaches or exceeds 26, you subtract 26. Carrying this through to the end (…h-e-i-t-s-v-o-r-l-e-s-u-n-g with the continuing key letters), you obtain the complete ciphertext of the model solution:

> **Ugdlvjakjlvywqvzzfjtgzyey**

**Why Vigenère defeats frequency analysis (task 3.3).** Because the same plaintext letter is encrypted with a *different* shift depending on its position, an E becomes sometimes this, sometimes that cipher letter. Look at the table: the e at position 4 becomes L, the e at position 10 also becomes L (coincidentally key position H again), but an e further back that falls on the key letter S would become W. The one tall E bar from the Caesar histogram is thus *smeared* across many letters. The ciphertext histogram becomes flat — and an attacker can no longer simply guess "tallest bar = E". That is the whole advance from monoalphabetic to polyalphabetic.

> **Memory aid:** Caesar = one alphabet, shifts the histogram. Vigenère = many alphabets, flattens the histogram. Transposition = the same histogram, different order.

## Exam focus

What is really tested here is handiwork under time pressure. Practise until it's solid: (1) letter ↔ number without thinking (A=0 … Z=25), (2) Caesar in both directions with the formula and the clean "mod 26" at the end, (3) encrypting **and** decrypting a columnar transposition — the stumbling block is always sorting the key and correctly reversing the permutation during decryption, (4) a few Vigenère positions with the cyclically repeated key written underneath. And be ready to explain in one sentence *why* frequency analysis kills Caesar and simple substitution, but fails against Vigenère and against transposition (for two completely different reasons!).
expl:cs-2025-u02:content:
This sheet has two faces. In the first half you compute the Vernam cipher — and in doing so discover why "perfect" encryption can nonetheless be dangerous in practice (keyword: malleability). In the second half you look under the hood of DES: S-boxes, Feistel network, confusion and diffusion. Both halves are exam-relevant, and both rely on exactly one operation you must master with confidence: the **XOR** (⊕). We go through each task one by one and compute every bit-step.

## Task 1 — Vernam cipher (one-time pad) by hand

Vernam is "Vigenère taken to its logical conclusion": the key is exactly as long as the plaintext. Each plaintext letter is added to *its own* key letter (mod 26). If the key is truly random and used only once, the method is called a **one-time pad** and is provably unbreakable. The rule is the old Caesar formula, just with a changing k: e(x) = (x + k) mod 26.

### Step by step — VORLESUNG with key SECUNIDUE

Convert letters to numbers (A=0 … Z=25), add position by position, and subtract 26 everywhere the result is greater than 25:

| Plaintext | V | O | R | L | E | S | U | N | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| x | 21 | 14 | 17 | 11 | 4 | 18 | 20 | 13 | 6 |
| Key | S | E | C | U | N | I | D | U | E |
| k | 18 | 4 | 2 | 20 | 13 | 8 | 3 | 20 | 4 |
| x + k | 39 | 18 | 19 | 31 | 17 | 26 | 23 | 33 | 10 |
| mod 26 | 13 | 18 | 19 | 5 | 17 | 0 | 23 | 7 | 10 |
| Ciphertext | **N** | **S** | **T** | **F** | **R** | **A** | **X** | **H** | **K** |

The ciphertext is **NSTFRAXHK**. Pay attention to the three positions where 26 is subtracted: 39→13, 26→0 (that becomes an A!), 33→7. That is exactly where it is decided whether you have understood the "mod 26".

**Task 1.2 — How many keys are there?** Each position can independently carry one of 26 letters. So for length 5: 26 · 26 · 26 · 26 · 26 = **26⁵**, and in general, for length n, just **26ⁿ**. That is the whole strength of the one-time pad: the key space grows exponentially with the message length.

**Task 1.3 — How long does brute force take?** Here you just carefully add up units. Given: 1 GHz = 10⁶ operations/s (as defined on the sheet), the CPU has 2 cores at 4 GHz each, and each key check costs 4 operations.

- Per core: 4 GHz = 4 · 10⁶ operations/s ÷ 4 operations per key = 10⁶ keys/s.
- Two cores: 2 · 10⁶ keys/s.
- Keys for length 1024: 26¹⁰²⁴ ≈ 8.56 · 10¹⁴⁴⁸.
- Time: 8.56 · 10¹⁴⁴⁸ ÷ (2 · 10⁶) ≈ 4.28 · 10¹⁴⁴² seconds ≈ **1.35 · 10¹⁴³⁵ years**.

The number is absurdly large — that is the point. Brute force against a full-fledged one-time pad is physically hopeless.

**Task 1.4 — Does double encryption help?** No, and that is surprising. If you encrypt twice, e_{k2}(e_{k1}(x)) = (x + k1 + k2) mod 26. If you set k3 = (k1 + k2) mod 26, that is exactly a *single* Vernam encryption with key k3. So the key space remains **26ⁿ** — no gain. The reason: addition mod 26 is "closed", two shifts together again yield exactly one shift. (With DES it is different, there multiple encryption pays off — hence Triple-DES.)

## Task 2 — Malleability of stream ciphers

A stream cipher encrypts with **y = x ⊕ s** (plaintext XOR keystream). Exactly from this a dangerous property follows: a cryptosystem is called **malleable** if an attacker can deliberately alter the ciphertext such that the plaintext changes in a controlled way — *without knowing the plaintext*.

**Task 2.1 — Why are stream ciphers always malleable?** Because XOR "passes through". If the attacker adds an arbitrary bit string Δ onto the ciphertext, then: y ⊕ Δ = (x ⊕ s) ⊕ Δ = (x ⊕ Δ) ⊕ s. On decryption, s drops out again and what remains is **x ⊕ Δ**. In other words: every bit the attacker flips in the ciphertext flips exactly the same bit in the plaintext. He has full control over the plaintext change, even though he never sees the plaintext.

### Step by step — Task 2.2 (Bitcoin broker)

The account balance (number of bitcoins) is stored as a number in the ciphertext. You receive 1 BTC (≈ €40,000) and want to become a millionaire, i.e. ≥ €10⁶. That is 10⁶ ÷ 40,000 = **25 BTC**. So you must crank the stored value up from 1 to 25 — via XOR, without knowing the keystream. You are looking for the Δ with 1 ⊕ Δ = 25:

| | Value | binary (5 bit) |
|---|---|---|
| current | 1 | 00001 |
| target | 25 | 11001 |
| Δ = 1 ⊕ 25 | 24 | **11000** |

Check: 00001 ⊕ 11000 = 11001 = 25. ✓ So you must XOR-add **11000** onto the ciphertext. The bit pattern Δ is 1 exactly where 1 and 25 differ.

### Step by step — Task 2.3 (Streaming, m → p, known-plaintext)

You know the plaintext (**m**), the corresponding ciphertext, and instead want to set the ciphertext for **p**. ASCII (7 bit): m = 109 = 1101101, p = 112 = 1110000. Given is the ciphertext of m: y(m) = 1000000.

**Step 1 — reconstruct the keystream.** Because you know the plaintext *and* the ciphertext (known-plaintext), you compute s = m ⊕ y(m):

    m   = 1 1 0 1 1 0 1
    y(m)= 1 0 0 0 0 0 0
    s   = 0 1 0 1 1 0 1   (XOR position by position)

**Step 2 — build the new ciphertext for p.** Now you encrypt p with the same keystream: y(p) = p ⊕ s:

    p   = 1 1 1 0 0 0 0
    s   = 0 1 0 1 1 0 1
    y(p)= 1 0 1 1 1 0 1

So you enter **1011101** into the database. (It can be done more briefly via the difference: Δ = m ⊕ p = 0011101, then y(p) = y(m) ⊕ Δ = 1000000 ⊕ 0011101 = 1011101 — the same result, because x ⊕ s = y and y ⊕ s = x are the two sides of the same XOR coin.)

> **Memory aid:** Stream cipher = XOR. And XOR is its own inverse: a ⊕ b ⊕ b = a. That is why an attacker can flip bits without knowing the key — that is the whole malleability attack.

## Task 3 — DES: key length, confusion & diffusion, Feistel

**3.1 — Key length.** Originally **128 bits** were proposed, in the end it was reduced to **56 bits** (effective) — at the insistence of the **NSA**. This short key length is exactly the reason why DES is attackable by brute force today and obsolete.

**3.2 — Confusion and diffusion** are the two principles (from Shannon) according to which every good block cipher is built:

- **Confusion** obscures the relationship between key and ciphertext — every part of the ciphertext should depend on the key in a complicated, inscrutable way. Building block in DES: the **S-boxes** (substitution).
- **Diffusion** spreads the influence of a single plaintext symbol over as many ciphertext positions as possible — if a plaintext bit is flipped, many ciphertext bits should change. Building block in DES: the **bit permutation**.

> **Memory aid:** Confusion = obscure key ↔ ciphertext (S-box). Diffusion = scatter one plaintext bit over many ciphertext bits (permutation).

**3.3 — Feistel network.** The block is split into two halves L and R. In each round i: the new left half is the old right (L_i = R_{i−1}), and the new right half is the old left XOR a function of the old right with the round key: R_i = L_{i−1} ⊕ f(R_{i−1}, k_i). The trick: because XOR is its own inverse, this round can be computed *backwards* **without** f having to be invertible — that is why decrypting and encrypting work with the same hardware building block, only the round keys run in reverse.

## Task 4 — Nonlinearity of S-box #1

An S-box would be **linear** if S(x1) ⊕ S(x2) = S(x1 ⊕ x2) held. Linear boxes would be easy to break; DES S-boxes are deliberately **nonlinear**. What is to be shown: the equation does *not* hold. For that you first need to know how to read an S-box at all.

### Step by step — how to read S-box #1

The input has 6 bits: b1 b2 b3 b4 b5 b6. The **outer** bits (b1 and b6) form the **row** (0–3), the **inner** four bits (b2 b3 b4 b5) form the **column** (0–15). The table value (decimal) is output as a 4-bit number. Example 011011: outer 0|1 → row 1, inner 1101 = 13 → column 13.

Now the three pairs. For each we compute on the left S(x1) ⊕ S(x2) and on the right S(x1 ⊕ x2) and show that the two are different:

**Pair 1: x1 = 000000, x2 = 000001.**
- S(000000): row 0|0 = 0, column 0000 = 0 → value 14 = 1110.
- S(000001): row 0|1 = 1, column 0000 = 0 → value 0 = 0000.
- Left: 1110 ⊕ 0000 = **1110**.
- x1 ⊕ x2 = 000001; S(000001) = 0000. Right: **0000**.
- 1110 ≠ 0000 → nonlinear. ✓

**Pair 2: x1 = 111111, x2 = 100000.**
- S(111111): row 1|1 = 3, column 1111 = 15 → value 13 = 1101.
- S(100000): row 1|0 = 2, column 0000 = 0 → value 4 = 0100.
- Left: 1101 ⊕ 0100 = **1001**.
- x1 ⊕ x2 = 011111; S(011111): row 0|1 = 1, column 1111 = 15 → value 8 = 1000. Right: **1000**.
- 1001 ≠ 1000 → nonlinear. ✓

**Pair 3: x1 = 101010, x2 = 010101.**
- S(101010): row 1|0 = 2, column 0101 = 5 → value 6 = 0110.
- S(010101): row 0|1 = 1, column 1010 = 10 → value 12 = 1100.
- Left: 0110 ⊕ 1100 = **1010**.
